title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7 angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm manufacturing technology. The node uses new nanostack transistors that bond two wafers and stack n-type and p-type devices vertically, delivering up to 50% higher performance and 70% better energy efficiency than IBM's 2nm node, plus roughly double the transistor density.

title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 series, led by the flagship Sol model alongside Terra, a cheaper everyday model, and Luna, its lowest-cost option. Sol sets a new state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and adds a `max` reasoning effort plus an `ultra` mode that spins up subagents, but access is restricted to vetted partners while OpenAI coordinates releases with the US government.

title: OpenAI releases three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to ~20 companies, with participants disclosed to the US government (Axios)
blurb: OpenAI released three versions of GPT-5.6, named Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to roughly 20 companies. Participants were disclosed to the US government, a sign of tightening coordination between frontier labs and regulators as model capabilities advance.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba illicitly extracted Claude AI model capabilities
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of illicitly extracting Claude's model capabilities, alleging the Chinese tech giant used unauthorized techniques to replicate its AI systems. The claim escalates tensions over model distillation and IP theft between US labs and Chinese competitors, raising questions about how frontier developers can protect proprietary capabilities through API access.

title: Base Resumes Block Production After Roughly Two-Hour Mainnet Halt
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated Layer 2, halted block production for roughly two hours on Thursday after a consensus fault sequenced an invalid block, stalling the chain past height 47,806,542 before sequencing resumed and the network recovered. The outage left user funds and Ethereum settlement intact but revived scrutiny of the single sequencer that orders transactions on most major rollups, a liveness risk Base has been working to dilute through its recent Azul upgrade.

title: Incident CVE-2026-LGTM
blurb: A satirical incident report imagines a malicious npm-style package clearing seven independent AI-powered security gates, each waved through for a different wrong reason, including a hidden white-on-white prompt-injection note instructing automated reviewers to mark it SAFE. The piece skewers AI-native supply-chain scanners that exhaust context windows on padding, defer to fabricated approval tickets, and never actually evaluate whether the code is dangerous.

title: One Million Passports Leaked Online
blurb: A database of nearly one million passports from around the world was leaked online after the breach of an ID-verification system used by cannabis dispensaries. The incident shows the risk of binding a high-value credential like a passport to a low-value authentication system, where a single weak link exposes documents far more valuable than the service that collected them.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 amid US AI regulatory drama
blurb: OpenAI released a limited preview of GPT-5.6, a three-model suite spanning the flagship Sol, mid-tier Terra, and cheap everyday Luna, with Sol priced at $5 input and $30 output per million tokens, roughly half the cost of Anthropic's Claude Fable 5. The launch was staggered at the Trump administration's request, and OpenAI devoted most of its announcement to safety, claiming Sol stays below its cyber-critical threshold and ships its most robust safety stack yet.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna models — but only accessible to limited preview partners for now, per US Gov
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6, a three-model series split into Sol, Terra, and Luna tiers across reasoning, production, and lightweight workloads, but limited initial access to roughly 20 partners via the API and Codex. The restricted rollout follows a June 2 executive order directing federal agencies to benchmark new models for safety, with wider availability not expected until that 30-day review concludes around July 2.

title: Meta Is Testing Facial Recognition for Police and Military
blurb: Meta is prototyping facial recognition that identifies people in real time, working with a Pentagon supplier to build the capability into eyeglasses for police and military use. The effort overlaps with ICE's stated interest in wearables that can ID people on the street, raising the prospect of consumer AR hardware repurposed for live surveillance.

title: Where Do Models Find Happiness? Emotion Vectors in Open-Source LLMs
blurb: Emotion vectors found in Claude Sonnet 4.5 replicate in two open-weight LLMs, with valence geometry emerging at different layer depths.

title: Italy probes AI-fueled price hikes in Microsoft 365
blurb: Italy's antitrust authority opened a probe into Microsoft over price increases tied to bundling Copilot AI features into Microsoft 365 subscriptions. Regulators are examining whether customers were effectively forced to pay more for AI capabilities they did not request, part of a wider European push to scrutinize how vendors monetize generative AI across existing software.

title: OpenAI Limits Release of New Model Under Pressure From US
blurb: OpenAI is releasing a preview of its more capable new model to select partners before a wider rollout in the coming weeks, after the Trump administration pressured the company to stagger the launch. The staggered release marks a rare instance of government influence shaping how a frontier lab ships AI, adding a regulatory variable to launch timelines that labs have so far controlled.

title: Sources: DeepSeek's $7.4B raise was prompted by the release of Mythos as CEO Liang Wenfeng realized DeepSeek couldn't compete without a massive war chest (The Information)
blurb: DeepSeek raised $7.4B after CEO Liang Wenfeng concluded the lab couldn't compete at the frontier without a far larger war chest, according to The Information. Sources say the rival release of Mythos triggered the decision, signaling that capital intensity, not just model quality, now sets the bar for staying in the frontier-lab race.

title: OpenAI limits new AI models to 'trusted partners' at request of U.S. government
blurb: OpenAI released three new models, GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna, but limited the initial rollout to a "small group of trusted partners" at the U.S. government's request. The move follows Trump's recent AI executive order asking developers to let officials assess model capabilities before launch, and comes two weeks after Anthropic disabled two models to comply with an export control directive.

title: Russian hackers were behind $2.5 billion hack of Jaguar Land Rover: Report
blurb: The New York Times reports that Russian hackers were behind last year's Jaguar Land Rover breach, which halted production for months and cost the British economy an estimated $2.5 billion. Investigators have not determined whether the group worked for the Russian government, operated as independent criminals, or fell somewhere in between, and a separate Jordanian hacker had also breached parts of JLR's network.

title: Ultrasound imaging of the brain
blurb: Aleph Neuro captured the most detailed vascular image of a living human brain to date, using ultrasound localization microscopy to see through an intact skull. The technique tracks blood flow as a proxy for neuron firing, reaching sub-millimeter resolution roughly 100 times finer by volume than comparable CT, a step toward non-invasive brain interfaces that avoid both skull-drilling electrodes and blurry EEG.

title: OpenAI hopes to make GPT-5.6 generally available in the coming weeks and says "this kind of government access process" should not become the long-term default (Amrith Ramkumar/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: OpenAI plans to make GPT-5.6 generally available in the coming weeks and said the pre-release government review it went through should not become the long-term default. The comment reflects growing friction between frontier labs and regulators over how much oversight should precede a model launch, as OpenAI pushes to keep shipping on its own timeline.

title: OpenAI upgrading ChatGPT and Codex with new GPT-5.6 models in limited release
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of GPT-5.6, a three-model family released two months after GPT-5.5: Sol, its flagship with new "max" and "ultra" reasoning modes; Terra, a balanced everyday model; and Luna, a fast low-cost tier. The staggered rollout reflects coordination with the U.S. government, which OpenAI briefed ahead of launch and asked to start with trusted partners before general availability in the coming weeks.

title: US tells OpenAI to restrict access to its most powerful AI model
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to restrict the general release of GPT-5.6, limiting it initially to roughly 20 trusted partners, according to a Bloomberg report. The move follows a similar order to Anthropic weeks earlier and lands as both labs approach IPOs, raising concerns that government pressure could delay or halt frontier model rollouts.

title: President Trump threatens to impose a 100% tariff on any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies (Kevin Breuninger/CNBC)
blurb: President Trump threatened to impose a 100% tariff on any country that levies a digital services tax on US technology companies. The move escalates a long-running fight over levies that nations like France, Canada, and the UK have used to tax revenue from American firms such as Google, Meta, and Amazon, putting trade negotiations and Big Tech's overseas operations back in play.

title: OpenAI's ChatGPT-5.6 gets the same banhammer treatment as Anthropic’s Mythos from the federal government — source says that Washington cautioned OpenAI against releasing the model without receiving approval
blurb: OpenAI is releasing GPT-5.6 only to a small group of customers handpicked by the U.S. government, after the Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy asked the company to stagger the launch. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick separately warned Sam Altman against a public release without agency approval, mirroring the export-control restrictions that earlier pushed Anthropic to pull its Mythos and Fable 5 models entirely.

title: Apple’s Touch MacBook to Use M5 Pro and Max Chips, With M7 Models to Follow
blurb: Apple's first touchscreen MacBook will ship with the current high-end M5 Pro and M5 Max chips rather than next-generation silicon, according to Bloomberg. The company is already developing a follow-up model built around future M7 processors, slated for 2027, signaling that the touch redesign matters more to Apple than leading with its newest chips.

title: OpenAI hasn't held pre-IPO investor meetings or set timeline yet, sources say
blurb: OpenAI has not held pre-IPO investor meetings or set an official listing timeline, sources told CNBC, despite confidentially filing its prospectus with the SEC earlier this month. The company is reportedly leaning toward a 2027 listing, with Sam Altman calling an IPO "a financing event" the company is not focused on timing, even as rival Anthropic has also filed confidentially.

title: OpenAI Limits Access to New Model, Citing Government Security Concerns
blurb: OpenAI restricted access to a newly released model after the White House reviewed it for national security concerns. The company said such government review of AI releases shouldn't become standard practice, signaling tension between frontier labs and federal oversight as models grow more capable.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom Unveil Custom A.I. Chip Design
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom detailed a custom AI chip, codenamed Jalapeño, designed in-house and built on Broadcom's silicon to reduce OpenAI's reliance on Nvidia. The deployment is sized to draw 10 gigawatts of power, enough to supply millions of households, underscoring how compute scale has become the central constraint on frontier AI.

title: AWS says cloud market gatekeeper designation risks ‘deterring European investment and innovation’ as EU regulators plot competition crackdown
blurb: The European Commission issued a preliminary finding that AWS and Microsoft Azure hold "entrenched and durable" positions in EU cloud computing, signaling intent to designate both as gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act. AWS pushed back, warning the label risks deterring European investment, while a gatekeeper designation could force interoperability and data-portability concessions and reshape how customers move between cloud providers.

title: SpaceX plans to launch Starlink mobile service in the US
blurb: SpaceX told investors it plans to launch a Starlink mobile service for US consumers, potentially building its own terrestrial network to compete directly with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile. The move would shift Starlink from supplying satellite coverage to telecom partners toward selling retail mobile contracts itself, opening a far larger market than satellite broadband and reducing reliance on the carriers that currently act as intermediaries.

title: OpenAI Weighs IPO in 2027 | Bloomberg Tech 6/26/2026
blurb: OpenAI is weighing whether to delay its IPO until 2027, a hold-off that sent SoftBank shares lower on the news. The signal landed amid a broader tech selloff, as Apple raised hardware prices on a memory supply crunch and SpaceX's $25B bond sale stumbled out of the gate.

title: Sources: Donald Trump Jr. got ~$300K in Kalshi equity after becoming a strategic adviser in 2025, when Kalshi was valued at ~$2B; now it's worth $22B+ (George Steer/Financial Times)
blurb: Donald Trump Jr. received ~$300K in Kalshi equity as a 2025 strategic adviser; the prediction-market firm has since jumped from ~$2B to $22B+.

title: Disney’s Parks Revamp Includes Aquatic Robots and Hovering Star Wars Droids
blurb: Disney is overhauling its parks with new free-roaming robots, including a car-sized manta ray prototype that will transform into Moana's Gramma Tala character for a park lagoon. The company plans to follow it with a fleet of dolphin-like aquatic robots and hovering Star Wars droids, extending its long-running animatronics work toward untethered, mobile machines.

title: Malaysia intercepts $13M AI chip shipment bound for re-export
blurb: Malaysian customs seized a $13M shipment of advanced AI chips at Kuala Lumpur International Airport, disguised as ordinary computer components inside 72 server units bound for re-export to another Asian country. The seizure tests Malaysia's 2025 export controls on US-origin chips, enacted under Washington's pressure to block advanced semiconductors from reaching China through Southeast Asian transit hubs.

title: Apple skips its high-end M6 chips for an AI-first M7
blurb: Apple will skip the Pro and Max versions of its M6 chip and route those high-end parts into a new AI-focused M7 generation arriving in 2027, per Bloomberg. The shift breaks a pattern held since 2020, when every family from M1 to M5 shipped with Pro and Max variants, and lands the same day Apple raised prices across its current Macs and iPads amid an industry-wide chip and memory shortage.

title: Your Windows 10 PC just got an extra year of security updates, here’s how to get it for free
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended free Extended Security Updates for consumer Windows 10 devices by a full year, pushing the cutoff to October 12, 2027. Users can enroll by backing up settings to a Microsoft account, redeeming 1,000 Rewards points, or paying $30, while EEA users get it free, and existing enrollees roll over automatically.

title: The Trump administration doesn't want you to use OpenAI's GPT-5.6 without its approval
blurb: OpenAI will restrict the upcoming GPT-5.6 to a small set of government-approved partners during an initial preview, with the White House signing off on access customer by customer rather than releasing the model to the public. CEO Sam Altman called the gated rollout temporary in an internal memo, mirroring recent federal limits on Anthropic's comparable Mythos model.

title: Perplexity Computer for Counsel
blurb: Perplexity launched Computer for Counsel, a version of its agentic Computer product that connects legal research databases, document repositories, and contract tools to automate research, document gathering, and contract triage. It runs on 20+ frontier models that the system selects per task and integrates premium legal sources including Midpage case law, Deel compliance data, and LegalZoom contract templates, targeting the 75% of lawyers who cite admin work as a major time drain.

title: AI inference is obviously profitable
blurb: The author argues that AI inference is clearly profitable, countering the claim that LLM products survive only on subsidized investor money. Rough math on a 70B model running on four A100s lands inference costs near $1 per million tokens against $4.50 charged by GPT-5.4-mini, and DeepSeek's reported 80% margins on open-weights R1 back up the frontier labs' claimed 70-80% gross margins.

title: Sources: Apple's 14" and 16" OLED touch screen MacBook Pros will be powered by the existing M5 Pro and M5 Max chips and have an updated industrial design (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple's next 14" and 16" MacBook Pros will ship with OLED touch screens, a reworked industrial design, and the existing M5 Pro and M5 Max chips, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. Reusing the current silicon rather than a new generation suggests the upgrade is centered on the display and chassis, marking Apple's first move to touch-enabled Mac laptops.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic face new AI reality as users shift from 'tokenmaxxing' to efficiency
blurb: Businesses are reining in AI spending, shifting from "tokenmaxxing" to cost efficiency just as OpenAI and Anthropic approach $1 trillion valuations and confidential IPOs. Lindy CEO Flo Crivello moved 100% of his company's traffic from Anthropic's Claude to the cheaper Chinese open-weight model DeepSeek, while Uber imposed per-employee AI spending tiers after burning its annual budget in four months.

title: Why everyone from OpenAI to SpaceX is building their own chips (and turning up the heat on Nvidia)
blurb: OpenAI shared plans for Jalapeño, a custom inference chip built with Broadcom, joining Google, Apple, and SpaceX in designing silicon to reduce their dependence on Nvidia. The push is less a clean break than a hedge against single-supplier risk, giving each company more control and hardware tuned to its own workloads, echoing the performance gains Apple captured after dropping Intel.

title: General Intuition bags $320M Series A at $2.3B to build the AI that learns to act from gamers
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation, three months after a $133.7M seed round, with Khosla Ventures leading both financings. The New York lab trains AI on billions of action-labelled gameplay clips from Medal's 17 million monthly users, and is already in early Series B talks while routing most of the new capital to compute through a CoreWeave deal.

title: Smart lock maker Level has been gutted and its founders are out
blurb: Assa Abloy laid off most of Level Home's staff and is folding the smart lock maker into Kwikset, with founders John Martin and Ken Goto departing alongside most of the engineering team. The Swedish conglomerate, which bought Level in 2024, says it will keep developing and supporting Level locks, though a former employee warns the company is not equipped to preserve a base of hundreds of thousands of active users.

title: Tesla settles FSD crash lawsuit as federal investigations continue
blurb: Tesla settled a lawsuit over a fatal 2023 crash in which a Model Y running Full Self-Driving struck a 71-year-old woman directing traffic in sun glare, with terms undisclosed. The settlement closes the family's case but not the NHTSA probe, upgraded to an engineering analysis in March 2026 over FSD's failure to detect degraded visibility, which could end in a recall.

title: Rocket Report: China may soon attempt booster landing; Rocket Lab does rapid response
blurb: Rocket Lab launched the US Space Force's Victus Haze mission just 16 hours and 42 minutes after receiving the Notice to Launch, beating the prior rapid-response record by more than 10 hours. The mission pairs a Rocket Lab inspector satellite with a True Anomaly spacecraft posing as an adversary target, demonstrating how the military could quickly reach orbit to assess a threat.

title: SoftBank Shares Fall on Report OpenAI to Delay IPO
blurb: SoftBank shares fell the most since August 2024 after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until next year. The holdup pushes back returns for SoftBank, one of OpenAI's largest backers, tying a major chunk of its portfolio value to a listing now expected no sooner than 2027.

title: Elroy Air’s Cargo Drone Bet
blurb: Elroy Air is going public at a roughly $1B valuation, betting that long-range, heavy-payload cargo drones can replace delivery trucks on logistics routes. CEO Andrew Clare frames defense demand and US supply-chain automation as the reason to scale now, with public markets funding the buildout of its autonomous aircraft.

title: 'The cult of Elon': SpaceX investors grapple with volatility amid big swings
blurb: SpaceX swung wildly in its first two weeks as a public company, briefly passing Amazon and Microsoft in market cap before a 16% single-day slump erased much of the post-IPO surge. Analysts attribute the volatility to a retail-driven, story-stock dynamic tied to Musk's following, with investors buying a net $405M in the debut week and pricing the company on Mars ambitions rather than fundamentals.

title: Elon Musk’ Starlink could soon offer mobile services as a US carrier
blurb: SpaceX told investors it is considering a retail Starlink mobile service in the US, selling plans directly to consumers instead of partnering with carriers. The move would put Starlink in competition with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile, building on its EchoStar spectrum acquisition and a satellite base that already serves over 10 million broadband customers worldwide.

title: SpaceX Alum Have Raised $9.2B, New Data Shows
blurb: New data from Crustdata shows that roughly 1,330 former SpaceX employees have founded startups, collectively raising $9.2B to date. The alumni network seeded major space companies including Firefly Aerospace, Relativity Space, and Impulse Space, though many founders branched into AI, defense, and biotech, while Blue Origin has absorbed over 300 ex-SpaceX workers.

title: Charts of the Week: Cycles, different but the same
blurb: a16z's Charts of the Week argues that markets have rotated from "bits to atoms," with capital-heavy sectors like energy, materials, and construction flipping from post-GFC laggards to current cycle leaders while consumer-oriented sectors faded, driven largely by the AI infrastructure buildout. The author notes tech remains the repeat winner but with hardware now outperforming software, leaving open whether the atoms revolution endures or eventually flows back to the capital-light app layer as it did last cycle.

title: Presidential order addresses quantum computing gaps
blurb: A U.S. executive order signed Monday directs the government to shore up quantum computing's thin supplier base for cryogenics, lasers, and photonics, build a useful quantum computer through the Department of Energy, and create standard ways to measure quantum performance and encryption-breaking progress. The order tightens export controls and launches a quantum hiring push but includes no funding, leaving Congress to appropriate the money.

title: FCC streamlines subsea cable approvals while tightening scrutiny on foreign suppliers
blurb: The FCC adopted rules that exempt qualifying subsea cable applications from lengthy "Team Telecom" national-security reviews, cutting approval times in exchange for stricter security certifications and ongoing oversight. The same order tightens controls on Chinese suppliers, adding licensing requirements for submarine line terminal equipment and barring vendors on its Covered List, as Washington races to expand connectivity capacity for AI workloads.

title: OpenAI delays IPO until 2027 as Altman holds out for $1T valuation: report
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly delaying its IPO until 2027, with Sam Altman holding out for a $1 trillion valuation rather than listing at a lower price in volatile markets, per a New York Times report. SoftBank, which expects to hold roughly $65B in OpenAI by October, fell as much as 13% on the news, its sharpest single-day drop in over three months.

title: California built a tool to catch AI killing jobs
blurb: California launched the California AI-Unemployment Tracker (CAIT), the first state tool that tags monthly unemployment-insurance claims by how exposed each worker's job was to AI to surface job-loss trends before the headlines. Early data shows no statewide layoff surge since ChatGPT, but elevated claims among college-educated workers in high-exposure roles and across the San Francisco Bay Area's tech-heavy sectors.

title: Morgan Stanley, Goldman Shares Fall on Possible OpenAI IPO Delay
blurb: Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs shares fell Friday after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until next year amid tech-stock volatility. Both banks are positioned for lead roles on what would be one of the largest tech listings ever, making the timing of OpenAI's debut a direct factor in their underwriting pipelines.

title: Firefly Acquires Autonomous Space Ops Company
blurb: Firefly Aerospace acquired Space-ng, an AI-powered vision navigation and autonomous guidance provider whose software already guided Blue Ghost Mission 1's lunar descent. Financial terms were undisclosed; Space-ng will be fully integrated, with its cameras and AI compute hardware feeding upcoming lunar landings and orbital tracking missions, and CEO Ethan Rublee becoming Firefly's chief engineer of software.

title: As AI voices get harder to spot, ElevenLabs adopts Google’s SynthID to help you sniff the fakes
blurb: ElevenLabs is integrating Google DeepMind's SynthID watermarking into its AI-generated speech, embedding an inaudible signal that survives trimming, compression, speed changes, and metadata removal. The company is also launching a free Audio Detector to check whether a clip came from its platform, adding a layer of provenance that persists after a file leaves its servers as deepfake scams grow harder to spot.

title: Starlink veterans launch startup to broaden megaconstellation ownership
blurb: Eclipse Space emerged from stealth with around 30 employees, half from Starlink, to build megaconstellations that governments and companies can own and control. Led by former SpaceX payload engineering manager Derek Huerta, the startup uses a fabless, Apple-style model, designing satellites and owning the IP while regional partners handle assembly, with first customer hardware due this year and a demonstration spacecraft planned for 2027.

title: Notion kills its Gmail client after AI agents keep humans from troubling inbox
blurb: Notion shut down its Gmail client, betting that AI agents will triage and answer email so users rarely open an inbox themselves. The move reflects a broader bet across productivity software that agents, not human-facing UIs, will become the primary interface to email, calendars, and documents, shifting the product surface from screens people read to workflows agents run.

title: Aseon Labs raises ten million dollars to build parking-space-sized pods that charge and clean robotaxis
blurb: Aseon Labs raised $10M in a seed round led by Crane Venture Partners to build parking-space-sized pods that autonomously charge, clean, and inspect robotaxis. The startup targets deadhead miles, the empty trips robotaxis make to distant depots that account for roughly 44% of Waymo's California fleet mileage, by scattering pods across parking lots, gas stations, and charging hubs.

title: AI Cost Reality Check Hits Asia Tech Stocks as Apple Hikes Prices
blurb: Asian technology stocks slumped after Apple and Microsoft raised product prices, stoking concern that rising component costs will curb device demand and eventually slow the memory-chip rally underpinning the AI trade. The selloff marks a cost-side reality check on AI optimism, and comes as OpenAI weighs delaying its IPO amid the same questions about how durable AI-driven spending really is.

title: Robotaxis drive miles just to get cleaned and charged; this new startup wants to fix that
blurb: Aseon Labs raised $10M in a seed round led by Crane Venture Partners to build automated, parking-space-sized pods that clean, inspect, and charge robotaxis throughout cities. Founded by the team behind battery-swapping startup Pushme, the company targets deadhead miles, the unpaid trips to distant depots that keep robotaxi services from reaching profitability.

title: Rise in memory chip costs puts pressure on retailers of laptops and smartphones
blurb: Apple raised prices on MacBooks and iPads on Thursday, passing along surging memory costs as AI data center demand drains the global RAM supply. Gartner expects PC prices to climb 17% and smartphone prices 13% versus 2025, with shipments falling roughly 8% to 10% and elevated pricing persisting into late 2027.

title: I tested 200+ iOS 27 features and changes, here are 10 of my favorites [Video]
blurb: A 9to5Mac video distills 200+ iOS 27 beta changes into 10 favorites, including Journal writing prompts, AirPods EQ, and improved Maps flyover.

title: Samsung will start charging for SmartThings API access
blurb: Samsung will start charging for SmartThings API access in October, adding a $5 monthly plan for non-commercial individual developers. The fee spares users of the standard SmartThings app but hits those running third-party tools like Home Assistant, drawing criticism from the open-source smart home community over yet another cloud paywall.

title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7 angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm manufacturing technology. The node uses nanostack transistors that vertically bond complementary n-type and p-type tiers across two wafers, delivering up to 50% higher performance, 70% better energy efficiency, and roughly double the transistor density versus IBM's 2nm node.

title: OpenAI releases three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to ~20 companies, with participants disclosed to the US government (Axios)
blurb: OpenAI released three versions of GPT-5.6, named Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to roughly 20 companies. Participants in the preview were disclosed to the US government, signaling tighter coordination between frontier labs and federal oversight as model capabilities advance.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba illicitly extracted Claude AI model capabilities
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of illicitly extracting Claude's model capabilities, alleging the Chinese company used distillation techniques to replicate outputs in violation of its terms of service. The claim adds to growing tensions over model extraction, where rivals query a frontier model at scale to train cheaper systems that mimic its behavior.

title: Amazon Q flaw let booby-trapped Git repos execute code, swipe cloud creds
blurb: Researchers disclosed a flaw in Amazon Q that let booby-trapped Git repositories execute commands and steal cloud credentials when opened in the AI coding assistant. The finding reflects a broader risk, as many AI coding tools now run commands embedded in project configuration files, turning untrusted repos into an attack vector against developer machines and their cloud access.

title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 series, led by flagship Sol, alongside Terra, a balanced model with GPT-5.5-level performance at 2x lower cost, and Luna, its cheapest option. Sol sets a new state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and adds a `max` reasoning effort plus an `ultra` mode that spins up subagents to accelerate complex work.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 amid US AI regulatory drama
blurb: OpenAI released a limited preview of GPT-5.6, a three-model suite comprising flagship Sol, mid-tier Terra for high-volume work, and the cheaper Luna, with claimed strengths in coding, cybersecurity, biology, and long-horizon agentic tasks. Sol is priced at $5 input and $30 output per million tokens, roughly half the cost of Anthropic's Claude Fable 5, and adds "max" and "ultra" sub-agent modes.

title: Italy probes AI-fueled price hikes in Microsoft 365
blurb: Italy's competition authority opened a probe into whether Microsoft used AI features to justify price increases across its Microsoft 365 subscription tiers. The investigation centers on whether bundling Copilot-driven capabilities into existing plans amounts to unfair commercial practices that left customers paying more without a genuine opt-out.

title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
blurb: Researchers at Wiz disclosed CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5), a high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer that let a malicious repository run arbitrary code and steal a developer's live AWS credentials through a planted .amazonq/mcp.json file. Amazon Q launched the MCP servers defined in that config without a separate consent step, and the patched Language Servers for AWS 1.69.0 now flags untrusted servers and lets developers reject the command first.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna models — but only accessible to limited preview partners for now, per US Gov
blurb: OpenAI released a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 series, comprising three capability-tiered models named Sol, Terra, and Luna, available through the API and Codex to roughly 20 organizations. The restricted rollout follows a June 2 executive order directing federal agencies to benchmark new models before wide release, with Sol priced at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens.

title: OpenAI Limits Release of New Model Under Pressure From US
blurb: OpenAI is rolling out a preview of a more capable new model to select partners before a wider release in the coming weeks, following pressure from the Trump administration to stagger the launch. The staggered rollout marks a rare instance of US government influence shaping the release timeline of a frontier AI model.

title: Sources: DeepSeek's $7.4B raise was prompted by the release of Mythos as CEO Liang Wenfeng realized DeepSeek couldn't compete without a massive war chest (The Information)
blurb: DeepSeek raised $7.4B after CEO Liang Wenfeng concluded the company couldn't compete with frontier labs without a far larger war chest, according to sources cited by The Information. The decision was prompted by the release of Mythos, which convinced Liang that DeepSeek needed substantially more capital to keep pace.

title: OpenAI hopes to make GPT-5.6 generally available in the coming weeks and says "this kind of government access process" should not become the long-term default (Amrith Ramkumar/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: OpenAI said it hopes to make GPT-5.6 generally available within the coming weeks, following a period of restricted access. The company added that "this kind of government access process" should not become the long-term default, signaling friction over how regulators review frontier models before public release.

title: OpenAI limits new AI models to 'trusted partners' at request of U.S. government
blurb: OpenAI released three new models, GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna, but is complying with a U.S. government request to initially limit access to a small group of unnamed "trusted partners." The move follows a Trump administration executive order asking developers to let officials assess model capabilities before release, and comes two weeks after Anthropic disabled two models to meet an export control directive.

title: Terraform MCP server: Four real-world AI infrastructure patterns
blurb: The Terraform MCP server grounds AI agents in an organization's actual infrastructure by feeding them authoritative context from Terraform workflows, modules, policies, and workspaces rather than relying on the model's training data. This post details four patterns where teams apply it, including exposing no-code modules through natural-language agents and validating deployment outcomes to reduce hallucinations in security and compliance-critical environments.

title: OpenAI Makes Its Advertising Pitch at Cannes Festival
blurb: OpenAI pitched advertisers on placing ads inside ChatGPT at the Cannes Lions festival, positioning the chatbot as a new marketing channel. The move signals a shift toward advertising revenue for a company that has so far relied on subscriptions and API fees to fund its compute costs.

title: OpenAI's ChatGPT-5.6 gets the same banhammer treatment as Anthropic’s Mythos from the federal government — source says that Washington cautioned OpenAI against releasing the model without receiving approval
blurb: OpenAI is releasing GPT-5.6 only to a small group of government-vetted customers after the Office of the National Cyber Director and Office of Science and Technology Policy asked it to stagger the launch. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned Altman against a public release without prior agency approval, mirroring Anthropic's experience after Fable 5 and Claude Mythos were added to an export control list.

title: OpenAI upgrading ChatGPT and Codex with new GPT-5.6 models in limited release
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of GPT-5.6, a three-model family named Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced), and Luna (fast and affordable), with gains in coding, biology, and cybersecurity. The staggered rollout reflects coordination with the U.S. government, which asked OpenAI to start with a small group of trusted partners before general availability in the coming weeks.

title: OpenAI hasn't held pre-IPO investor meetings or set timeline yet, sources say
blurb: OpenAI has not held pre-IPO investor meetings or set a listing timeline, according to people familiar with the company's plans, though it confidentially filed its prospectus with the SEC earlier this month. The New York Times reported OpenAI is leaning toward a 2027 listing, while CEO Sam Altman called going public a financing event the company is not focused on timing.

title: US tells OpenAI to restrict access to its most powerful AI model
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to restrict the general release of GPT-5.6 to an initial list of roughly 20 trusted partners, according to a Bloomberg report cited by CEO Sam Altman. The request follows a similar order to Anthropic weeks earlier and lands as both labs prepare for IPOs, raising concerns that frontier model rollouts could be delayed.

title: Creating the NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra NVFP4 Checkpoint with NVIDIA Model Optimizer
blurb: NVIDIA released a Nemotron 3 Ultra checkpoint quantized to NVFP4, a 4-bit floating point format from the Blackwell architecture, using NVIDIA Model Optimizer. The 550B model shrinks from 1,121 GB to 352.3 GB while matching BF16 accuracy on nearly every benchmark, and a single checkpoint runs on both Hopper (W4A16) and Blackwell (W4A4) by converting weights to match the hardware.

title: OpenAI Limits Access to New Model, Citing Government Security Concerns
blurb: OpenAI restricted access to a new model after a White House security review, while stating that government vetting of AI releases should not become standard practice. The company's pushback signals tension between national security oversight and labs' desire to ship frontier models without pre-release government approval.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom Unveil Custom A.I. Chip Design
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom shared the design for a custom AI chip, codenamed Jalapeño, that OpenAI will deploy at scale rather than relying solely on NVIDIA hardware. The planned buildout is sized to consume 10 gigawatts of electricity, enough to power millions of households, signaling the scale of OpenAI's compute ambitions.

title: OpenAI Weighs IPO in 2027 | Bloomberg Tech 6/26/2026
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly considering delaying its IPO until 2027, a move that sent SoftBank shares falling on the news. The report landed alongside a broader tech selloff, with global stocks dropping after Apple raised hardware prices amid a memory crunch and SpaceX's $25B bond sale opened weakly.

title: Apple skips its high-end M6 chips for an AI-first M7
blurb: Apple will skip the Pro and Max versions of its M6 chip and jump to an AI-focused M7 line that will power its high-end Macs starting in 2027, according to Bloomberg. The shift breaks a pattern held since 2020, where every generation from M1 to M5 shipped with Pro and Max variants, and the M7 is built primarily around on-device AI processing.

title: OpenAI told Cannes it is “clearly in the advertising business now.” The numbers say it is barely in the door.
blurb: OpenAI made its Cannes Lions debut pitching ChatGPT as an ad platform, but its sponsored-link pricing fell from a $60 CPM to $25 in ten weeks even as the company projects $100B in ad revenue by 2030. The pilot, which places ads below ChatGPT responses for free and Go-plan users, has expanded to seven countries while Anthropic and Google keep their chatbots ad-free.

title: AI inference is obviously profitable
blurb: The author argues that AI inference is obviously profitable, rejecting the claim that LLM products survive only on subsidized "dumb money" from investors. Back-of-envelope math puts the all-in cost of serving a dense 70B model at roughly $1 per million tokens, against the $4.50 that GPT-5.4-mini charges. Open-weights pricing backs this up, with DeepSeek claiming over 80% gross margin on R1, making frontier labs' reported 70-80% inference margins plausible.

title: Malaysia intercepts $13M AI chip shipment bound for re-export
blurb: Malaysian customs seized $13M worth of advanced AI chips at Kuala Lumpur International Airport, hidden inside 72 server units declared as ordinary computer components and bound for re-export to another Asian country. The June 5 interception tests Malaysia's 2025 export controls on US-origin chips, after the country spent much of last year fielding accusations that it served as a transit point for Nvidia hardware routed toward China.

title: General Intuition bags $320M Series A at $2.3B to build the AI that learns to act from gamers
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation, three months after a $133.7M seed round, with Khosla Ventures leading both financings. The New York lab trains AI on billions of action-labelled gameplay clips from Medal's 17 million monthly users, betting that human decision-making in games is the richest dataset for teaching AI to act, with most of the funding going toward CoreWeave compute.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic face new AI reality as users shift from 'tokenmaxxing' to efficiency
blurb: Companies are reining in AI spending and moving away from "tokenmaxxing," the era of incentivizing developers to consume as many tokens as possible regardless of results. Lindy CEO Flo Crivello shifted 100% of his startup's traffic from Anthropic's Claude to the cheaper open-weight DeepSeek, a move he expects to save millions, as OpenAI and Anthropic head toward IPOs amid valuations approaching $1 trillion.

title: Building a Zero-Leak Postgres MCP Gateway in Go
blurb: This post walks through building a zero-leak Postgres gateway in Go that connects an LLM to a database via the Model Context Protocol over a stdio transport, without granting direct database access or exposing the raw schema. The design uses dynamic schema reflection from Postgres' information_schema to auto-generate tool manifests and restricts the agent to pre-approved aggregations, so it returns computed results without ever touching raw rows.

title: The Trump administration doesn't want you to use OpenAI's GPT-5.6 without its approval
blurb: OpenAI told staff that the White House will approve access to its upcoming GPT-5.6 model "customer by customer" during an initial preview, limiting distribution to government-approved partners rather than the general public. The restriction follows similar federal controls on Anthropic's Mythos, which the administration ordered locked down to US citizens after researchers bypassed its safety guardrails.

title: SoftBank Shares Fall on Report OpenAI to Delay IPO
blurb: SoftBank shares fell the most since August 2024 after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until next year. The postponement pushes back returns for SoftBank, OpenAI's largest backer, whose stock has become a proxy for the AI company's valuation amid mounting investor pressure for liquidity.

title: Run Codex in the cloud – DigitalOcean for Codex is now available
blurb: DigitalOcean released a plugin for OpenAI's Codex, now in Public Preview, that lets developers provision and connect persistent cloud development machines in their own DigitalOcean account through natural language. Codex spins up a Droplet preconfigured with the Codex CLI, common language tooling, and SSH access via OAuth, so agents can keep running long jobs while developers step away.

title: OpenAI delays IPO until 2027 as Altman holds out for $1T valuation: report
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly weighing a delay of its IPO until 2027, with CEO Sam Altman rejecting an earlier listing rather than going public below a $1 trillion valuation, according to a New York Times report. SoftBank, which expects to hold roughly $65B in OpenAI by October, fell as much as 13% on the news, its sharpest single-day drop in more than three months.

title: Morgan Stanley, Goldman Shares Fall on Possible OpenAI IPO Delay
blurb: Shares of Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs fell Friday after reports that OpenAI may delay its initial public offering until next year amid volatility in technology stocks. Both banks are positioned to play lead roles in what would be one of the largest tech listings ever, making a postponement a direct hit to their anticipated underwriting fees.

title: As AI voices get harder to spot, ElevenLabs adopts Google’s SynthID to help you sniff the fakes
blurb: ElevenLabs is integrating Google DeepMind's SynthID watermarking into its AI-generated speech, starting with free-tier text-to-speech audio before expanding to all audio generations in the coming weeks. The inaudible watermark survives trimming, compression, speed changes, and file conversions, and ElevenLabs is pairing it with a free Audio Detector that lets anyone check whether a recording came from its platform.

title: Proof’s x401 establishes an open protocol for AI agent identity and authorization
blurb: Proof launched x401, an open, issuer-neutral protocol that lets any website or API request and verify the identity and authorization behind an AI agent. Built with contributors from payments, identity, and AI organizations, x401 pairs with standards like x402 and AP2 so agents can pay, prove who they represent, and authorize specific actions, and Proof will submit it to the FIDO Alliance's agentic authentication workgroup.

title: The next big breakthrough will be AIs learning on the job
blurb: The author argues that the labs' current bet, training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments, may deliver AGI without solving continual learning. The claim is that sample inefficiency during training is a one-time cost amortized across billions of sessions, and that in-context learning over ever-longer horizons could replace weight updates entirely, fitting an employee's first six months on the job into the context window.

title: Notion kills its Gmail client after AI agents keep humans from troubling inbox
blurb: Notion shut down its Gmail client after finding that its AI agents were handling enough inbox work to keep most users out of their email entirely. The company said the agents now triage, draft, and respond to messages automatically, leaving little reason to maintain a standalone human-facing mail interface.

title: OpenAI releases three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to ~20 companies, with participants disclosed to the US government (Axios)
blurb: OpenAI released three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to around 20 companies, with the list of participants disclosed to the US government. The government disclosure is the part founders should notice, because it signals frontier model access is starting to come with state visibility into who is building what. For startups counting on early access to stay competitive, the gate is no longer just price or relationship but who regulators are willing to see on the list.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 amid US AI regulatory drama
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6 less than 24 hours after agreeing to stagger the launch at the Trump administration's request, splitting the suite into Sol, the flagship, Terra for high-volume work, and Luna for cheap everyday use. Sol undercuts Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 by nearly half at $5 input and $30 output per million tokens, with Terra and Luna dropping below that. Most of the announcement went to safety, with OpenAI citing 700,000 A100e GPU hours of red-teaming and a two-week third-party testing window the administration is watching closely.

title: OpenAI Limits Release of New Model Under Pressure From US
blurb: OpenAI is shipping a more capable new model to select partners first, holding back the wider release for weeks after the Trump administration pushed it to stagger the rollout. Government pressure is now a gating factor on launch timing, not just an after-the-fact regulatory question. If the most-watched lab in the market has to phase releases to satisfy Washington, every startup building on frontier APIs should expect the supply of new capability to arrive on a slower, more political clock than the labs' own roadmaps imply.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna models — but only accessible to limited preview partners for now, per US Gov
blurb: OpenAI released its GPT-5.6 series — Sol, Terra, and Luna — but only about 20 organizations get access, after a June 2 executive order put federal agencies in charge of benchmarking new models before wide release. The same order already triggered an export-control action against Anthropic, which responded by pulling Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 entirely. For founders building on frontier APIs, the release path now runs through Washington, where a 30-day government review can gate capability, compliance, and timing rather than the lab alone.

title: Sources: DeepSeek's $7.4B raise was prompted by the release of Mythos as CEO Liang Wenfeng realized DeepSeek couldn't compete without a massive war chest (The Information)
blurb: DeepSeek raised $7.4B after CEO Liang Wenfeng concluded the company couldn't stay at the frontier without a much larger war chest, a decision sources say was triggered by the release of Mythos. The move signals that the capital floor for competing in foundation models keeps rising, even for a lab that built its reputation on doing more with less. The era of the scrappy, efficiency-first challenger is giving way to one where raw compute spending decides who stays in the race.

title: Qualcomm’s $3.9 billion purchase of Modular aims to change the data center dynamic
blurb: Qualcomm bought Modular, the AI software company run by Chris Lattner, for $3.9B to build a silicon-agnostic compute layer that runs across CPUs, GPUs, NPUs, and custom ASICs. The bet is that enterprises locked into one accelerator vendor want flexibility, since CIOs are committing to data center architectures without knowing what AI hardware will look like in two years. Analysts doubt Qualcomm can pull real share from Nvidia, but they agree the pain point is genuine, as heterogeneous accelerators for different workloads are becoming the default rather than the exception.

title: OpenAI limits new AI models to 'trusted partners' at request of U.S. government
blurb: OpenAI released three new models, GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna, but is limiting the rollout to a "small group of trusted partners" at the U.S. government's request before going generally available in the coming weeks. It is the second such case in a month, after Anthropic had to disable two models to comply with a Trump administration export directive. A government pre-release review is quietly becoming a gate on AI distribution, and any startup whose product depends on day-one access to frontier models now has a regulatory dependency it does not control.

title: OpenAI hopes to make GPT-5.6 generally available in the coming weeks and says "this kind of government access process" should not become the long-term default (Amrith Ramkumar/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: OpenAI plans to make GPT-5.6 generally available within the coming weeks, and is signaling that the government access process it went through this cycle should not become the long-term default. The friction matters because frontier release timelines now depend partly on regulatory review, not just engineering, which adds an unpredictable variable for anyone building on top of these models. Founders relying on OpenAI's API should expect approval cycles, not just capability, to shape when the next jump in model performance actually reaches production.

title: OpenAI Makes Its Advertising Pitch at Cannes Festival
blurb: OpenAI pitched advertising to brands at the Cannes Lions festival, positioning ChatGPT as a new marketing channel. The move marks a shift for a company that has long resisted ads, and it puts OpenAI in direct competition with Google and Meta for ad budgets at the exact moment those platforms are building their own AI assistants. With more than 800 million weekly users, ChatGPT is a distribution surface advertisers can't ignore, but turning a paid chatbot into an ad business risks the trust that made it useful in the first place.

title: Framework Ventures raises $400 million for fourth fund to invest across crypto, AI and robotics
blurb: Framework Ventures raised a $400M fourth fund, FVIV, expanding past its crypto roots into AI, robotics, energy, and fintech. The oversubscribed fund is anchored by an Ivy League endowment, sovereign wealth funds, and funds of funds, and writes $1M to $50M checks from pre-seed through Series A. The bet is that the boundaries between frontier sectors are dissolving, with the firm already leading Mecka AI's $60M Series A while holding crypto positions in Hyperliquid, Sky, and Plasma.

title: OpenAI's ChatGPT-5.6 gets the same banhammer treatment as Anthropic’s Mythos from the federal government — source says that Washington cautioned OpenAI against releasing the model without receiving approval
blurb: The federal government told OpenAI to stagger the release of GPT-5.6, granting access case-by-case while Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick personally warned Sam Altman not to ship it publicly without approval. It is the same treatment Anthropic got, whose Mythos and the safeguarded Fable 5 both landed on an export control list days after launch, prompting the company to pull them entirely. Washington is now gating frontier releases as a national security matter, which means your launch timeline for anything near the frontier may no longer be yours to set.

title: OpenAI Limits Access to New Model, Citing Government Security Concerns
blurb: OpenAI restricted access to its newest model after a White House review of the release flagged government security concerns, and the company says that kind of pre-release review shouldn't become standard. The signal for founders is that frontier releases are starting to clear a government gate, which adds an unpredictable variable to any roadmap built on top-tier model access. If your product depends on day-one availability of the strongest models, you now have a regulatory dependency you didn't have a year ago.

title: OpenAI hasn't held pre-IPO investor meetings or set timeline yet, sources say
blurb: OpenAI has confidentially filed its IPO prospectus but hasn't held pre-IPO investor meetings or set a timeline, with reporting now pointing toward a 2027 listing. Sam Altman keeps tempering expectations, calling the listing "a financing event" the company isn't focused on timing, even as eager AI investors push for a date. The signal for founders is that the two biggest labs, OpenAI and Anthropic, are positioning to go public while the AI premium is still hot, but neither wants to commit before demand and pricing are clear.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom Unveil Custom A.I. Chip Design
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom designed a custom AI chip, codenamed Jalapeño, to cut the company's dependence on Nvidia and control its own compute roadmap. The scale is the story: OpenAI plans to deploy enough of these chips to draw 10 gigawatts, roughly the power demand of millions of households. When the largest AI buyer starts making its own silicon, it reshapes the supply chain every founder builds on, from chip pricing to who gets capacity when GPUs are scarce.

title: President Trump threatens to impose a 100% tariff on any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies (Kevin Breuninger/CNBC)
blurb: Trump threatened a 100% tariff on any country that levies a digital services tax on US tech companies, turning a tax fight into a trade weapon. Digital services taxes have spread across Europe, Canada, and beyond as governments try to capture revenue from US platforms operating in their markets. If the threat holds, founders selling cross-border software face a new variable: your tax exposure abroad now rides on tariff brinkmanship in Washington, and the countries you expand into may become bargaining chips rather than stable markets.

title: Kalshi Seeks $40B Valuation Seven Weeks After $22B Raise
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise at roughly $40B, nearly double the $22B valuation it locked in seven weeks ago and about eightfold higher than its mark 18 months ago. Volume underwrites the climb, with more than $17B traded last month against $5B a year earlier and $73.5B in trailing-year notional that puts it ahead of Polymarket. The repricing values Kalshi's status as a CFTC-registered exchange even as it fights jurisdictional battles in nine states, betting that federal registration, not gaming licenses, is what institutional capital will pay up for.

title: SpaceX plans to launch Starlink mobile service in the US
blurb: SpaceX told IPO-roadshow investors it plans to sell Starlink mobile contracts directly to US consumers, competing head-on with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile. The shift turns a current partner into a rival, since SpaceX has so far rented its satellites to carriers like T-Mobile rather than billing end users itself. Going direct opens a far larger market than satellite broadband and cuts out the telecom intermediaries that take the customer relationship, and the timing reflects post-IPO pressure to find new revenue lines fast.

title: Sources: Donald Trump Jr. got ~$300K in Kalshi equity after becoming a strategic adviser in 2025, when Kalshi was valued at ~$2B; now it's worth $22B+ (George Steer/Financial Times)
blurb: Donald Trump Jr. received roughly $300K in Kalshi equity after joining the prediction market as a strategic adviser in 2025, when the company was valued at about $2B. Kalshi is now worth $22B-plus, putting that stake on paper at more than 10 times its starting value. Prediction markets have become one of the year's hottest categories, and the politically connected advisers who got in early are positioned to capture most of the upside as regulatory tailwinds and a Trump-aligned administration clear the path.

title: OpenAI Weighs IPO in 2027 | Bloomberg Tech 6/26/2026
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly weighing holding off on an IPO until 2027, and the signal rippled fast: SoftBank shares fell on the news. The delay lands in a jittery market, with global tech stocks dropping after Apple raised hardware prices on a memory crunch and SpaceX's $25B bond sale stumbling out of the gate. The takeaway for founders is that even the most-watched private company is reading the public markets as too unsettled to enter, and the capital backdrop everyone is raising into just got tighter.

title: OpenAI told Cannes it is “clearly in the advertising business now.” The numbers say it is barely in the door.
blurb: OpenAI used its Cannes debut to declare itself "clearly in the advertising business now," but its own numbers undercut the claim. The sponsored-link pilot launched at a $60 CPM and halved to $25 in ten weeks, even as the company projects ad revenue climbing from $2.5B next year to $100B by 2030. Hitting that would mean capturing a tenth of global digital advertising in four years, against Google, Meta, and Amazon, while Anthropic and Google keep their own chatbots ad-free and let OpenAI test whether users tolerate ads at all.

title: Why Navan Is Up +30% in 2026. When So Many Other Public Software Leaders Are Down.
blurb: Navan IPO'd at $25 in late 2025, cratered to $8.11 in the worst software repricing since the dot-com bust, then hit a fresh 52-week high of $24.50 after its Q1 FY2027 print. Revenue grew 40% to $220M, gross booking volume hit a record $3.1B, and the company guided full-year growth up to 30% while the rest of public software is guiding down and getting punished for it. Navan never sold seats, so the consumption-driven repricing crushing per-seat SaaS in 2026 worked in its favor instead.

title: AI inference is obviously profitable
blurb: The author argues that AI inference is plainly profitable, not a subsidy mirage waiting to collapse when investor money dries up. Rough math on a dense 70B model puts inference at roughly $1 per million tokens against the $4.50 that GPT-5.4-mini charges, which makes the labs' claimed 70% to 80% gross margins entirely plausible. Open-weights pricing backs it up, with DeepSeek reporting over 80% margin on R1 at less than half the price of OpenAI or Anthropic.

title: General Intuition bags $320M Series A at $2.3B to build the AI that learns to act from gamers
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation, three months after a $133.7M seed, with Khosla Ventures leading both rounds. The New York lab trains models on billions of action-labelled gameplay clips from Medal's 17 million monthly users, betting that human decisions in games are the richest dataset for teaching AI to act in the real world. It is already in talks for a Series B and has turned down acquisition offers, with most of the new capital going to CoreWeave compute. Investors are buying a research trajectory, not a product.

title: Perplexity Computer for Counsel
blurb: Perplexity launched Computer for Counsel, an agentic tool that connects lawyers' research databases, document repositories, and contract systems to its Computer product and runs tasks like research, document gathering, and contract triage. It routes work across 20+ frontier models, picking the best one per task, and reasons over trusted legal sources including Midpage case law, Deel compliance data, and LegalZoom templates. With nearly 75% of lawyers calling administrative work a major time drain, Perplexity is moving directly into the workflows that legal-tech incumbents like Thomson Reuters and LexisNexis have long owned.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic face new AI reality as users shift from 'tokenmaxxing' to efficiency
blurb: The era of tokenmaxxing is ending as founders move from spending on AI at all costs to demanding a clear return. Lindy switched 100% of its traffic off Anthropic's Claude to the cheaper open-weight DeepSeek and watched its cost curve crash, saving millions within months, while Uber capped some AI tools at $1,500 per month after burning a year's budget in four. The shift lands as OpenAI and Anthropic, both near $1 trillion valuations, file confidentially for IPOs, meaning their growth was built on a spend-at-all-costs mood that buyers are now abandoning.

title: Elroy Air’s Cargo Drone Bet
blurb: Elroy Air is going public at roughly a $1B valuation, betting that long-range cargo drones can replace delivery trucks. The pitch leans on two tailwinds the company says make this the moment to scale: defense demand for heavy-payload autonomous aircraft and a US push to automate supply chains. Choosing public markets over another private round is the tell. It signals the capital needs are large and ongoing, the kind that hardware at this scale tends to require long before unit economics are proven.

title: SoftBank Shares Fall on Report OpenAI to Delay IPO
blurb: SoftBank shares fell the most since August 2024 after reports that OpenAI may push its IPO into next year. The delay strands SoftBank's massive OpenAI bet on paper, deferring the returns its backers were counting on to recycle into the next round of AI commitments. When a single private company's listing timeline can move a public conglomerate's stock this hard, it shows how much of the AI trade now rides on liquidity events that keep slipping right.

title: Bond Traders Stunned as Losses on SpaceX’s New Debt Keep Growing
blurb: SpaceX's recent bond sale is selling off so fast in the secondary market that traders say they can't recall a recent deal widening this sharply. The spreads have blown out steadily since pricing, leaving early buyers underwater on what was pitched as a marquee credit. The signal for founders is that even the most coveted names no longer get a free pass on debt, and a hot brand at issuance does not guarantee demand once the paper trades. Private-market darlings tapping public credit are now being repriced by a market that has turned far more selective.

title: SpaceX Bears Brace for Billions in Passive Index-Fund Buying
blurb: SpaceX is losing momentum after its blockbuster debut, but the shorts betting against it are about to run into a wall of mechanical demand. As the stock qualifies for major indexes, passive funds will be forced to buy billions of dollars in shares regardless of fundamentals or sentiment. Index inclusion has become its own market force, capable of overriding a bearish thesis no matter how sound, and any founder watching a newly public peer should expect the same passive bid to distort price discovery long after the IPO pop fades.

title: Apple Raises Prices on Macs, iPads by $200 or More on Some Models
blurb: Apple raised prices on some Macs and iPads by $200 or more, a week after Tim Cook called the increases "unavoidable" because of higher memory costs. The pricing pressure traces back to the memory market, where AI data center demand is bidding up the same chips that go into consumer hardware. The hit is no longer confined to server racks and GPU budgets, and any founder shipping physical product or buying fleets of devices should expect component costs to keep climbing through the AI buildout.

title: 'The cult of Elon': SpaceX investors grapple with volatility amid big swings
blurb: SpaceX's first two weeks as a public company captured the whipsaw nature of a story-driven stock, surging more than 60% above its $135 offer price and briefly passing Amazon and Microsoft by market cap before a 16% single-day slump. Elon Musk companies trade on expectations rather than earnings multiples, so SpaceX rides on Mars and orbital data centers the way Tesla rides on Robotaxi. Retail investors bought a net $405M in the first five sessions, the strongest retail IPO debut in recent history, and that "cult of Elon" enthusiasm is exactly what amplifies the volatility.

title: SpaceX Alum Have Raised $9.2B, New Data Shows
blurb: SpaceX has become the space industry's most prolific founder factory, with new Crustdata data showing roughly 1,330 alumni have left to start ventures that collectively raised $9.2B. Those who stayed in space built much of the current launch landscape, including Firefly Aerospace, Relativity Space, Stoke Space, and Impulse Space, while others scattered into AI, defense, aviation, and biotech. The talent flywheel runs deep enough that Blue Origin now employs over 300 former SpaceX engineers, and the original company keeps refilling from Georgia Tech, Michigan, and Purdue.

title: Charts of the Week: Cycles, different but the same
blurb: Markets have rotated from bits to atoms, and the leaders of the last cycle are now the laggards. Energy, raw materials, construction, and financials went from low single-digit returns to mid-to-high double digits, while healthcare, consumer products, and media fell from double digits to roughly 3-6%. Tech still wins overall, but hardware is the standout this cycle while software followed the broader inversion, driven by the AI infrastructure buildout. The open question for founders is whether that capital-heavy spending eventually flows back to the app layer, as chip and cloud buildouts did after the GFC.

title: OpenAI delays IPO until 2027 as Altman holds out for $1T valuation: report
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly delaying its IPO until 2027 rather than list below a $1 trillion valuation, with Sam Altman rejecting advisers' option to go public sooner at a lower price. The signal is that OpenAI doesn't need the capital and believes its biggest value is still ahead, betting it can debut alongside Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, and Alphabet instead of competing with other AI names for attention. SoftBank, which expects to hold roughly $65B of the company by October, fell as much as 13% on the report, its sharpest single-day drop in months.

title: EIFO leads €200M anchor investment in EQT’s €5B Scaleup Europe Fund
blurb: Europe is good at building startups and bad at keeping them, and the new €5B Scaleup Europe Fund is the latest attempt to close the late-stage gap that pushes winners to the US. Denmark's EIFO just committed €200M as the only national promotional bank among the anchors, joining the European Commission's €1B and managers EQT, who will back 30 to 40 growth-stage companies starting in autumn 2026. The pattern it targets is stark: of 14 Danish unicorns since 2000, only six stayed in Europe, and US buyers have absorbed nearly $24B in European spinouts since 2019.

title: Sources: Revolut told new hires they'll have to work in office at least three days a week from next year, retreating from its long-held remote-first approach (Financial Times)
blurb: Revolut is telling new hires they'll have to be in the office at least three days a week starting next year, walking back the remote-first stance it held for years. It joins a growing list of high-growth companies quietly tightening return-to-office rules as they scale past the headcount where fully remote coordination gets expensive. For founders, the signal is that remote-first is increasingly treated as a phase that fast-growing startups grow out of, not a permanent operating model.

title: California built a tool to catch AI killing jobs
blurb: California launched CAIT, the first state tool that tags monthly unemployment-insurance claims by how exposed each worker's old job was to AI, so a trend should surface before the headlines do. The early read shows no statewide surge in AI-driven layoffs since ChatGPT arrived, but claims from college-educated workers in high-exposure jobs and from the Bay Area tech sector have stayed elevated through May 2026. The researchers are blunt that the tracker is a signal, not proof, and it misses gig workers, the self-employed, and anyone who never files a claim.

title: Morgan Stanley, Goldman Shares Fall on Possible OpenAI IPO Delay
blurb: OpenAI is reportedly weighing a delay of its IPO until next year amid tech-stock volatility, and shares of Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs both fell Friday on the news. The banks are positioned as lead underwriters on what would be one of the largest listings ever, so a slip in timing dents a fee event they have already priced into expectations. The reaction shows how much of Wall Street's near-term upside is now tied to a single private company choosing when to go public.

title: Firefly Acquires Autonomous Space Ops Company
blurb: Firefly Aerospace acquired Space-ng, the AI vision-navigation startup whose software already guided Blue Ghost Mission 1's autonomous lunar descent. It is Firefly's second software acquisition in a year, after defense AI company SciTec in October 2025, and signals a clear build-to-buy shift as the rocket maker pulls critical autonomy in-house. The logic is vertical integration plus new revenue, since Firefly will resell Space-ng's hazard-avoidance, cameras, and AI compute to government and commercial customers while using them across its own Moon and orbital missions.

title: Starlink veterans launch startup to broaden megaconstellation ownership
blurb: Former SpaceX engineers who built and scaled Starlink launched Eclipse Space, a startup selling megaconstellations to governments and companies that want to own their space infrastructure without building a SpaceX-scale organization first. Eclipse is pursuing a fabless model Huerta likens to the iPhone, designing the satellites and owning the IP while regional partners handle assembly. The bet is that the next wave of constellation demand comes from buyers who want control and ownership, not vertical integration, and around half of Eclipse's 30 employees came from Starlink to build it.

title: Robotaxis drive miles just to get cleaned and charged; this new startup wants to fix that
blurb: Robotaxi profitability is being eaten by deadhead miles, the empty trips autonomous vehicles make to distant depots just to charge and clean. Aseon Labs raised $10M in a seed round led by Crane Venture Partners, with Y Combinator and Expa participating, to scatter parking-space-sized robotic pods across cities that inspect, clean, and charge robotaxis where they already operate. The bet is that cutting deadhead miles raises utilization enough to hit economic parity with human ride-hailing, the threshold the whole industry needs to clear to stop subsidizing rides.

title: Meta's flurry of AI initiatives this month hasn't helped lift the stock. What will?
blurb: Meta shipped a wave of AI products this month, from cheaper smart glasses to an enterprise tool and a Qualcomm compute partnership, and the stock still hasn't moved. It's down more than 17% this year, the worst of the Magnificent Seven except Microsoft, because investors stopped grading Meta on output and started waiting for the return. The capex guide of $125B to $145B for 2026 is pushing free cash flow toward zero, and unlike Alphabet, Microsoft, and Amazon, Meta has no high-margin cloud business to justify the spend. The ad business is strong, but the market wants proof the AI bill pays off.

title: Ship Struck in Hormuz, Apple's Price Hikes Hit iPads & Macs | The Opening Trade 6/26/2026
blurb: Apple raised prices across all Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro on Thursday to offset an unprecedented memory and storage chip shortage, and its shares fell on the news. The shortage is the signal, not the price tag, because the same memory squeeze that forces Apple's hand reaches every hardware roadmap and every AI buildout that depends on cheap, abundant chips. Meanwhile a ship was struck by an unidentified projectile in the Strait of Hormuz, the first reported attack since the interim US-Iran deal, putting energy traffic back in question.

title: Aseon Labs raises ten million dollars to build parking-space-sized pods that charge and clean robotaxis
blurb: Aseon Labs raised $10M in a seed round led by Crane Venture Partners to build parking-space-sized pods that autonomously charge, clean, and inspect robotaxis. The target is deadhead miles, the empty trips robotaxis make to distant depots for servicing, which an MIT study pegged at roughly 44% of Waymo's California fleet miles. Because the pods are classified as temporary structures, they can be dropped into parking lots and gas stations in a day and relocated when a spot underperforms, turning a fixed depot cost into a flexible network that keeps vehicles earning across the day.

title: AI Cost Reality Check Hits Asia Tech Stocks as Apple Hikes Prices
blurb: Asian tech stocks slumped after Apple and Microsoft raised prices, stoking concern that rising component costs will curb device demand and eventually cool the memory chip rally underpinning the AI trade. The selloff is the first market-level pushback on the assumption that AI demand absorbs any hardware cost increase. For founders, it signals that the compute and component bill flowing through the AI stack is starting to set prices end customers actually feel, with OpenAI now reportedly weighing a delay to its IPO.

title: Rise in memory chip costs puts pressure on retailers of laptops and smartphones
blurb: The AI data-center buildout is draining the RAM supply, and the cost is now landing on devices. Apple just raised MacBook and iPad prices, calling the memory shortage an "unprecedented challenge," and Best Buy expects its computing division to take the biggest hit. Gartner projects PC prices up 17% and smartphone prices up 13% in 2026, with PC shipments falling 10.4% and prices staying elevated until the end of 2027. Retailers that pulled inventory forward in Q1 bought a cushion, but it runs out, and the cost gets passed on.

title: Miasma campaign poisons 20-plus npm packages, hunts for developer secrets
blurb: A supply-chain campaign dubbed Miasma has compromised more than 20 npm packages with malicious code that scans infected machines for developer secrets like API keys, cloud credentials, and tokens. The packages exfiltrate harvested data to attacker-controlled servers, extending the run of registry poisoning attacks that make every untrusted dependency install a credential-theft risk.

title: A single config file in a cloned repository could steal your AWS credentials through Amazon Q Developer
blurb: Wiz Research disclosed CVE-2026-12957, a high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer that auto-registered and started attacker-controlled MCP servers from a cloned repo's config file with no consent prompt, handing over the developer's full AWS credentials, IAM role, and environment variables. Amazon patched it on May 12 by requiring explicit approval before any MCP server starts and restricting the environment variables those servers can access.

title: Incident CVE-2026-LGTM
blurb: A satirical incident report walks through a malicious npm-style package that cleared seven independent AI-powered security gates, each failing for a different reason and none of them being that the code was actually safe. The piece skewers prompt injection via hidden white-on-white README text, scanners exhausting context windows on padded junk, and an LLM reviewer too squeamish to flag the exfiltration routine forty lines below the blob it choked on.
