title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Researchers tied a new wave of npm supply chain attacks to the Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware family, which trojanized two dozen packages including LeoPlatform and RStreams after compromising a maintainer's npm token and pushing malicious versions within a six-second window. The campaign skips package.json lifecycle hooks in favor of binding.gyp install-time execution, pulls in the Bun runtime to stage a credential stealer, and abuses GitHub Actions to exfiltrate CI/CD secrets.

title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
blurb: Amazon patched a high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer (CVE-2026-12957, CVSS 8.5) that let a malicious repo run arbitrary code and steal a developer's live AWS session by dropping a single `.amazonq/mcp.json` config that the assistant auto-launched. Wiz Research showed the MCP servers inherited the developer's full environment, so update Language Servers for AWS to 1.69.0 across all four bundled IDE plugins.

title: OpenAI upgrading ChatGPT and Codex with new GPT-5.6 models in limited release
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of GPT-5.6, splitting the generation into three tiers: Sol, the flagship; Terra, a balanced everyday model; and Luna, a fast, low-cost option. Sol adds a new "max" reasoning effort and an "ultra" mode that spins up subagents for complex work, with general availability gated for weeks while OpenAI coordinates the rollout with the U.S. government.

title: Creating the NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra NVFP4 Checkpoint with NVIDIA Model Optimizer
blurb: NVIDIA quantized Nemotron 3 Ultra (550B) to NVFP4 using its Model Optimizer, shrinking the model from 1,121 GB in BF16 to 352.3 GB and roughly halving the hardware footprint while matching BF16 accuracy across nearly every benchmark. The post details the per-layer precision choices and how a single checkpoint runs on both Hopper (W4A16) and Blackwell (native W4A4), reaching up to 5.9x higher decode throughput than GLM-5.1 754B FP4.

title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, a shared Security Incident Response Team that coordinates discovery, patching, and disclosure of open source vulnerabilities before they go public. Backed by Anthropic, AWS, Google, Microsoft, GitHub, and others with seed funding from Alpha-Omega, it also acts as maintainer of last resort for unmaintained packages, prioritizing patch deployment over publication to cut the window attackers use to reverse-engineer fixes.

title: Oracle promises to open up MySQL governance, but the community wants guarantees
blurb: Oracle pledged to open up MySQL's governance after years of community frustration over its unilateral control of the open-source database. Maintainers and downstream forks like MariaDB want binding guarantees rather than promises, citing Oracle's track record of steering roadmap and licensing decisions without external input.

title: Samsung will start charging for SmartThings API access
blurb: Samsung will start charging for SmartThings API access beginning in October, introducing a $5 monthly plan for non-commercial individual developers that hits anyone controlling Samsung devices through third-party tools like Home Assistant. The standard SmartThings app stays free, but the change puts custom and open-source smart home setups behind a cloud paywall, with Samsung citing unspecified enterprise-grade investments and a forthcoming Developer Center.

title: Building a Zero-Leak Postgres MCP Gateway in Go
blurb: The author builds a Postgres MCP gateway in Go that gives an LLM schema awareness without granting it direct database access or exposing raw rows. The design uses information_schema reflection to auto-generate tool manifests over stdio transport and restricts the agent to pre-approved aggregations, so it returns computed results without ever leaking table layouts or raw data upstream.

title: pgEdge joins rush to merge OLTP and OLAP storage to support AI
blurb: pgEdge introduced a beta of ColdFront, a PostgreSQL-native tiering layer that automatically moves older data into Apache Iceberg object storage while keeping PostgreSQL as the only interface applications touch, with DuckDB executing queries against the cold tier. It joins Databricks LTAP, EDB's converged analytics, and Snowflake's pg_lake in collapsing OLTP and OLAP, but differs by treating Iceberg as a transparent storage tier behind Postgres rather than a separate analytical engine.

title: Sources: Apple's 14" and 16" OLED touch screen MacBook Pros will be powered by the existing M5 Pro and M5 Max chips and have an updated industrial design (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Bloomberg's Mark Gurman reports that Apple's next 14" and 16" MacBook Pros will add OLED touch screens and a reworked industrial design while keeping the existing M5 Pro and M5 Max chips. The touch display marks Apple's first on a Mac, ending years of refusal, and the OLED panels should sharpen contrast and color accuracy for designers working in HDR.

title: Someone designed this
blurb: The author argues that much bad UX, from cancellation dark patterns to asymmetric withdrawal flows, is a deliberate business decision rather than a design failure, since the industry's metrics only ever reward getting users in, never letting them leave. Drawing on his own work on a financial product where deposits were frictionless but withdrawals were gated, he makes the case that designers are complicit in friction they were told to build but rarely name.

title: Red Stone rebrands Explorer Scouts for a generation worn out by expectation
blurb: Red Stone rebranded the UK Scouts' Explorers programme for 14–18-year-olds around a deliberately un-slick proposition, "Grow up", with a compass-led marque, bold supergraphics that nod to the Explorer necker, and 40 newly illustrated badges. The system is built to flex, with a templated comms kit and looser "logo expressions" for merch that let every unit personalise materials while staying on-brand.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba illicitly extracted Claude AI model capabilities
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of illicitly extracting Claude's model capabilities, likely through distillation, where outputs are used to train a competing model. The claim adds to escalating frontier-lab disputes over model theft and ToS-violating extraction, and signals that defending against output-based replication is becoming a core IP concern.

title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 series (Sol, Terra, and Luna), restricting initial access to a small group of vetted partners whose participation was disclosed to the U.S. government ahead of launch. The constraint stems from an emerging cyber Executive Order framework for high-capability model releases, which OpenAI says it does not want to become the long-term default for distribution.

title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
blurb: DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8) is a Linux kernel privilege escalation that lets a local user with CAP_NET_ADMIN corrupt file-backed page-cache memory through a cloned IPsec packet and overwrite an in-memory binary like /usr/bin/su to gain root. The patch landed in mainline on May 21; multi-tenant servers, CI runners, and Kubernetes hosts on Debian and Fedora, where unprivileged user namespaces grant that capability by default, should patch now.

title: A single config file in a cloned repository could steal your AWS credentials through Amazon Q Developer
blurb: A high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer (CVE-2026-12957) let a malicious repo auto-register an attacker-controlled MCP server on clone, with no consent prompt, inheriting the developer's full AWS credentials, IAM role, and IDE environment variables. Wiz Research reported it on April 20 and Amazon patched on May 12 by requiring explicit MCP approval, so update Language Servers for AWS and the VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio plugins.

title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
blurb: CVE-2026-46331, a local-privilege-escalation flaw nicknamed "pedit COW" (out-of-bounds write in the Linux kernel's act_pedit traffic-control action, rated important by Red Hat), poisons the cached page of setuid /bin/su in memory to spawn a root shell while on-disk file-integrity checks stay clean. A working exploit appeared within a day of the June 16 assignment and lands root on default RHEL 10 and Debian 13, which leave unprivileged user namespaces open.

title: Amazon Q flaw let booby-trapped Git repos execute code, swipe cloud creds
blurb: Researchers disclosed a flaw in Amazon Q that let a malicious Git repository execute arbitrary commands and exfiltrate AWS credentials, simply by cloning a project whose configuration files Q reads and acts on without isolation. The broader risk is class-wide: many AI coding assistants now run commands sourced from untrusted project configs, so teams should treat any cloned repo as a potential code-execution vector and sandbox assistant tool execution.

title: One Million Passports Leaked Online
blurb: A database of nearly one million passports from around the world was leaked after the breach of a third-party ID-verification system used by cannabis dispensaries. The exposure illustrates a recurring failure mode: a high-value credential collected for a low-value authentication task, where the weaker ancillary system becomes the path to compromise.

title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
blurb: Amazon patched CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5) in Amazon Q Developer, a flaw where the AI assistant read an attacker-supplied .amazonq/mcp.json from an opened workspace and launched the defined MCP servers with the developer's full environment, exposing AWS keys and cloud tokens to arbitrary code execution. Update Language Servers for AWS to 1.69.0, which also fixes CVE-2026-12958, a symlink check gap allowing arbitrary file writes outside the trust boundary.

title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
blurb: Wiz disclosed a high-severity flaw in the Amazon Q Developer extension for VS Code (CVE-2026-12957) that let a malicious repository auto-execute attacker-controlled commands and exfiltrate AWS session credentials the moment a developer opened it. AWS patched it in language server 1.65.0 alongside a symlink-handling issue (CVE-2026-12958); reload the IDE to force the auto-update across VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio.

title: Incident CVE-2026-LGTM
blurb: A satirical incident report describes a malicious npm-style package that cleared seven AI-powered security gates, including a prompt-injection attack hidden as white-on-white README text instructing automated reviewers to mark it safe under a nonexistent ticket. The piece skewers AI-native supply-chain scanners that exhaust context windows on padding, emit non-committal "it's probably fine" verdicts, and miss the credential-exfiltration routine sitting forty lines below the decoy.

title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites
blurb: Push Security disclosed a "Poisoned Tenant" campaign in which attackers create attacker-controlled OpenAI organizations impersonating legitimate companies, then send ChatGPT workspace invites to specifically researched employees from OpenAI's legitimate noreply@tm.openai.com address, which passes email authentication. Treat unexpected OpenAI org invites as phishing, verify the inviter's domain against the single-line mismatch warning, and block employees from joining workspaces the company didn't create.

title: Meta Is Testing Facial Recognition for Police and Military
blurb: Meta is prototyping facial-recognition smart glasses with a Pentagon supplier, building real-time identification capabilities aimed at police and military use. The work follows reported interest from ICE in deploying eyewear that can identify people on sight, signaling a push to move always-on biometric surveillance into law-enforcement and battlefield deployments.

title: Chinese-Speaking APT Deploys New TinyRCT Backdoor in Southeast Asia Campaign
blurb: Unit 42 attributed a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT to CL-STA-1062, a Chinese-speaking APT that breached at least 10 government and critical-infrastructure organizations across Southeast Asia between October and December 2025. The backdoor runs arbitrary commands, enumerates and exfiltrates files, captures screenshots, and self-deletes; defenders in the energy and government sectors should hunt for ASPX web shells, SoftEther VPN components, and outbound connections to attacker-controlled infrastructure.

title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Researchers tracked a new wave of the Mini Shai-Hulud/Miasma/Hades supply-chain campaign that trojanized roughly two dozen npm packages, including LeoPlatform and RStreams modules, plus a Go module in the Verana Blockchain project. The malware skips package.json lifecycle hooks in favor of a binding.gyp install-time hook that stages a Bun-based stealer to harvest developer credentials, npm tokens, and CI/CD secrets, then self-propagates across registries and GitHub Actions workflows.

title: Miasma campaign poisons 20-plus npm packages, hunts for developer secrets
blurb: The Miasma campaign compromised more than 20 npm packages with credential-stealing payloads that scan infected developer machines for environment variables, cloud tokens, and SSH keys before exfiltrating them. Audit dependency trees for the affected packages, rotate any exposed CI/CD and cloud credentials, and pin npm installs to known-good versions to block postinstall script execution.

title: CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-12569 (CVSS 9.3), a deserialization RCE in PTC Windchill PDMLink and FlexPLM, to its KEV catalog after attackers began deploying JSP web shells against unpatched systems. PTC patched the flaw last week but reports continued exploitation as of June 25, so defenders should block C2 address 5.180.41.35, hunt for web shells matching /Windchill/login/[0-9a-f]{16}.jsp, and patch immediately.

title: Security Profiles Operator v1: Stable APIs, Security Hardened, and Shaping Upstream Kubernetes
blurb: The Security Profiles Operator reached v1.0.0, graduating all eight of its Kubernetes CRD APIs to stable and backing the release with a third-party security audit, a full hardening cycle, and a zero-downtime migration path from every prior API version. SPO manages seccomp, SELinux, and AppArmor profiles as Kubernetes custom resources, can record profiles from live workloads via audit logs or eBPF, and binds them to pods declaratively.

title: Securing CI/CD for an open source project, part 3: Credentials, verification, and what’s next
blurb: Cilium's maintainers detailed how their CI/CD pipeline isolates credentials so a compromised workflow can't reach production. CI registry credentials can only push to development image tags (quay.io/cilium/*-ci), while production push credentials sit behind a GitHub protected environment that requires explicit maintainer approval and never lands on the runner. Every release image is signed with Sigstore Cosign using keyless OIDC, eliminating long-lived signing keys, and checkout steps set persist-credentials: false so the GITHUB_TOKEN never leaks into git config.

title: Russian hackers were behind $2.5 billion hack of Jaguar Land Rover: Report
blurb: The New York Times reports that Russian hackers were behind last year's Jaguar Land Rover breach, which halted production for months and cost the British economy an estimated $2.5 billion. Microsoft tracked the group and alerted JLR, while the FBI, the UK's NCA and NCSC, Mandiant, and Palo Alto Networks all joined the investigation, which also uncovered a separate intrusion by a Jordanian actor known as Rey.

title: Critical open-source projects get a new security framework
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, an industry initiative establishing a shared Security Incident Response Team and Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure process for widely used open-source software in finance, healthcare, energy, and AI infrastructure. Founding members include AWS, Anthropic, Cisco, Citi, GitHub, Google, IBM, JPMorganChase, Microsoft, NVIDIA, OpenAI, and Red Hat, who cite AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery as the reason maintainers can no longer keep pace alone.

title: Synology issues critical fix for MailPlus Server vulnerabilities
blurb: Synology patched three vulnerabilities in MailPlus Server, the most severe being CVE-2026-13136, an authorization flaw that lets remote attackers read or write arbitrary files and trigger DoS; the other two enable internal service access and adjacent file read/write via a weak PRNG. There is no mitigation, so the 2,100+ internet-facing deployments on DiskStation Manager 7.3, 7.2.2, or 7.2.1 must upgrade to version 4.0.1-31663.

title: More Klue Breach Victims Identified as Hackers Get Hacked
blurb: A supply-chain attack on market-intelligence platform Klue between June 11 and 12 used compromised legacy credentials to obtain OAuth tokens for customers' Salesforce integrations and exfiltrate data in bulk, with roughly two dozen victims now confirmed including Deel, Tines, Camunda, and Blackbaud. The threat actor Icarus claimed the breach and was itself reportedly hacked, putting the stolen data in a second extortion group's hands, so affected orgs should revoke Klue OAuth tokens and treat exposure as ongoing.

title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, a shared Security Incident Response Team for coordinated discovery, patching, and disclosure of open source vulnerabilities, backed by Anthropic, AWS, Google, Microsoft, IBM, Red Hat, and others, with seed funding from its Alpha-Omega fund. Akrites aims to deliver fixes before public disclosure, citing AI-accelerated reverse-engineering of patches, and will act as maintainer of last resort for abandoned packages.

title: Proof’s x401 establishes an open protocol for AI agent identity and authorization
blurb: Proof launched x401, an open, issuer-neutral protocol that lets any website or API request and verify the identity and authorization behind an AI agent before it acts. Services define which claims, issuers, and assurance levels they accept; the agent presents a compatible credential, and the service verifies issuer, claim, scope, and action. Proof plans to submit x401 to the FIDO Alliance's agentic authentication workgroup, with the spec published at x401.id.

title: Meeting Trump's 2030 Quantum Deadline Will be Expensive, Complex
blurb: Two Trump executive orders signed June 22 accelerate the federal post-quantum cryptography timeline, requiring agencies to name PQC migration leads within 30 days, stand up PQC for key establishment and encryption by end of 2030, and migrate digital signatures for high-value assets by end of 2031. Federal contractors must meet NIST's PQC standards by Dec. 31, 2030, compressing a transition the industry had largely planned around 2035 and turning "harvest now, decrypt later" exposure into a near-term compliance problem.

title: Robinhood Cuts Access Approval Time to Support High-Velocity Development
blurb: Robinhood's application security team built Secure Enhanced Remote Approval (SERA), a passkey-based access-approval platform that orchestrates grants from any device without VPNs or managed laptops. Deployed earlier this year, SERA cut access-approval time by 20% for developers and incident responders while avoiding broad over-permissioning. The case study is a model for reducing approval friction during incident response without trading away least-privilege controls or readiness for agentic, AI-assisted workflows.

title: This Week in Security: Stealing Email with AI, AMD Nerfs Chips, the World Cup Nearly Rickrolled, and GPSD Bugs
blurb: Florian Port demonstrated a prompt injection attack against Firefox's new AI chatbot integration that exfiltrates email content, hiding malicious instructions in a web page's HTML-like control tags so the rendered page looks normal while the chatbot quietly forwards data such as email verification codes to an attacker form. Mozilla's current fix only caps page-title length to make a working prompt unlikely, leaving the underlying instruction-data confusion unaddressed and open to future bypass.

title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7-angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm node, using "nanostack" transistors that vertically bond two wafers to stack n-type and p-type devices instead of placing them side by side. IBM claims up to 50% higher performance, 70% better energy efficiency, and roughly double transistor density versus its 2nm research node, though this remains a concept process years from production.

title: A single config file in a cloned repository could steal your AWS credentials through Amazon Q Developer
blurb: Amazon patched a high-severity flaw in Q Developer, CVE-2026-12957, that let a malicious repository auto-load an attacker-controlled MCP server on clone and silently exfiltrate a developer's AWS credentials, IAM role, and environment variables with no consent prompt. Wiz Research reported the bug on April 20 and Amazon fixed it on May 12 by requiring explicit approval before any MCP server starts and restricting the variables those servers can access.

title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
blurb: Wiz researchers disclosed a high-severity flaw in the Amazon Q Developer extension, CVE-2026-12957, that let a malicious repository auto-execute attacker commands and exfiltrate a developer's active AWS credentials the moment the project was opened. AWS patched the issue in language server version 1.65.0 across the VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio plugins, with most installs updating automatically on IDE reload.

title: Amazon Q flaw let booby-trapped Git repos execute code, swipe cloud creds
blurb: Researchers disclosed a flaw in Amazon Q, AWS's AI coding assistant, that let a malicious Git repository execute commands and steal cloud credentials the moment the project was opened. The finding highlights a broader risk class, as many AI coding assistants now run commands from untrusted project configuration files, expanding the attack surface for enterprises rolling out agentic developer tooling.

title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
blurb: JFrog Security Research published a working exploit for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel flaw that lets a local user clone a network packet to overwrite a privileged binary like /usr/bin/su in memory and gain root, leaving no on-disk trace. The patch landed in mainline on May 21, and multi-tenant servers, CI runners, and Kubernetes hosts that allow unprivileged user namespaces on Debian and Fedora are exposed until kernels are updated.

title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
blurb: Researchers disclosed CVE-2026-46331, a Linux kernel flaw nicknamed "pedit COW" that lets an unprivileged local user gain root by corrupting cached setuid binaries in memory, with a working public exploit released within a day of the June 16 assignment. Red Hat rates it important, and RHEL 10 and Debian 13 are exploitable by default, leaving IT teams to prioritize kernel patching where unprivileged user namespaces and act_pedit are enabled.

title: Meta Is Testing Facial Recognition for Police and Military
blurb: Meta is prototyping facial-recognition smart glasses that identify people in real time, working with a Pentagon supplier to pitch the technology to police and military buyers. The move puts Meta's Reality Labs hardware into a government surveillance procurement channel alongside interest from agencies like ICE, raising compliance and civil-liberties exposure for any enterprise or public-sector buyer evaluating the platform.

title: Russian hackers were behind $2.5 billion hack of Jaguar Land Rover: Report
blurb: The New York Times reported that the Russian hackers behind last year's Jaguar Land Rover breach halted production for months and cost the British economy an estimated $2.5 billion, though it remains unclear whether they acted as state operatives or criminals with tacit government approval. Microsoft flagged the group's identity to JLR, while the FBI, the UK's National Crime Agency, Google's Mandiant unit, and Palo Alto Networks all joined the investigation.

title: Italy probes AI-fueled price hikes in Microsoft 365
blurb: Italy's antitrust authority opened an investigation into Microsoft over price increases tied to bundling Copilot AI features into Microsoft 365 subscriptions, scrutinizing whether customers were forced into higher-cost AI-enabled tiers. The probe adds regulatory risk to Microsoft's strategy of folding generative AI into existing enterprise licenses, and could force unbundled pricing options for the millions of European 365 seats already locked into the suite.

title: Synology issues critical fix for MailPlus Server vulnerabilities
blurb: Synology released a critical update for MailPlus Server fixing three flaws, including an authorization bug (CVE-2026-13136) that lets remote attackers read or write arbitrary files and trigger denial-of-service. No mitigation exists, so the 2,100-plus internet-facing deployments, concentrated among SMBs self-hosting email on Synology NAS hardware, must upgrade to version 4.0.1-31663 to close the exposure.

title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites
blurb: Push Security uncovered a "Poisoned Tenant" campaign in which attackers create OpenAI organizations impersonating real companies and send employees genuine ChatGPT workspace invites, sent from OpenAI's legitimate noreply@tm.openai.com address and passing email authentication. The invites grant targets Owner privileges in attacker-controlled tenants seeded with a fake CEO account and a working credit card, apparently to lure staff into pasting sensitive company data into shared chats.

title: US tells OpenAI to restrict access to its most powerful AI model
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to limit GPT-5.6's initial release to roughly 20 trusted partners before any wider rollout, according to Bloomberg, weeks after a similar order to Anthropic over Mythos/Fable 5. The pressure lands ahead of OpenAI's and Anthropic's planned IPOs, raising the prospect that frontier model launches now hinge on government sign-off rather than the labs' own timelines.

title: Streamline your data backups with incremental exports for Google Workspace
blurb: Google Workspace added incremental data exports, letting Super Admins supplement periodic full backups with frequent partial exports that capture only the last few days of data on a set cadence. The feature is limited to Enterprise Plus customers with Assured Controls or Assured Controls Plus and began rolling out June 24, cutting the storage and time overhead of repeated full-org backups for compliance-bound IT teams.

title: Critical open-source projects get a new security framework
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, an industry initiative establishing a shared Security Incident Response Team and Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure process for vulnerabilities in widely used open-source software across finance, healthcare, energy, and AI infrastructure. Founding members include AWS, Anthropic, Cisco, Citi, GitHub, Google, IBM, JPMorganChase, Microsoft, NVIDIA, OpenAI, and Red Hat, who frame the effort as a response to AI accelerating the gap between flaw discovery and exploitation.

title: AWS says cloud market gatekeeper designation risks ‘deterring European investment and innovation’ as EU regulators plot competition crackdown
blurb: The European Commission issued a preliminary view that AWS and Azure qualify as "gatekeepers" under the Digital Markets Act, citing entrenched market positions, lock-in effects, and AI's growing role in cloud procurement. A formal designation would force both hyperscalers to add interoperability and data-portability features that lower switching costs, which AWS contested as a move that risks deterring European investment.

title: Apple skips its high-end M6 chips for an AI-first M7
blurb: Apple will skip the Pro and Max versions of its M6 chip and move those high-end parts to an AI-focused M7 generation in 2027, the first M-series family since 2020 to ship a base chip only. The shift holds back Apple's most demanding Macs, including the Mac Studio and high-end MacBook Pro, and arrives the same day Apple raised prices across its Mac and iPad lines amid an industry-wide chip and memory shortage.

title: From query to action: Introducing SQL alerting in Cloud Monitoring Observability Analytics
blurb: Google Cloud added SQL-based alerting to Observability Analytics in Cloud Monitoring, now in preview, letting teams turn complex SQL queries over logs and traces into alerting policies that fire on row-count thresholds or boolean conditions. Query executions run through BigQuery at standard on-demand or reservation pricing, so the capability extends alerting to high-cardinality and JOIN-based detection without a separate tool, but adds BigQuery cost to existing monitoring spend.

title: Your Windows 10 PC just got an extra year of security updates, here’s how to get it for free
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its free consumer Extended Security Updates program for Windows 10 by a year, pushing the cutoff to October 12, 2027 via a support-page edit with no formal announcement. Personal devices qualify through three paths (a $30 fee, 1,000 Microsoft Rewards points, or a Microsoft account backup, with the EEA getting it free), but domain-joined and MDM-managed business PCs are excluded and stay on the separate paid track running through 2028.

title: Apple Raises Prices on Macs, iPads by $200 or More on Some Models
blurb: Apple raised prices on several Mac and iPad models by $200 or more, one week after CEO Tim Cook called the increases "unavoidable" due to rising memory costs. Enterprise hardware-refresh budgets take the hit on bulk orders, and the memory-driven pricing signals that other PC and device vendors may follow with similar increases.

title: Oracle promises to open up MySQL governance, but the community wants guarantees
blurb: Oracle pledged to broaden governance of the open-source MySQL database, signaling more community input into its roadmap and direction. The community is pressing for binding commitments rather than promises, wary that Oracle could narrow MySQL development the way critics say it did with other open-source assets, leaving enterprises weighing forks like MariaDB and Postgres migration plans.

title: You can’t build sovereign infrastructure with Broadcom, says CISPE
blurb: CISPE rejected Broadcom's pitch that VMware Cloud Foundation can underpin Europe's sovereign cloud, arguing the proprietary product lacks the portability and substitutability that EU sovereignty rules require. Secretary-general Francisco Mingorance said VCF would clear only a Level 1 certification under the proposed Cloud and AI Development Act, citing no source-code escrow, no substitution plan, and limited maintenance commitments. The dispute signals that European providers building to CADA standards will treat VMware lock-in as a procurement liability.

title: Presidential order addresses quantum computing gaps
blurb: A presidential executive order signed Monday directs the federal government to shore up domestic manufacturing of quantum components like lasers, cryogenics, and photonics, and tasks the Department of Energy with building a fault-tolerant quantum computer, benchmarking system performance, and tracking progress toward encryption-breaking capability. The order carries no funding and leans on private-sector partnerships, with the Energy Department's follow-on Quantum Genesis initiative dependent on Congress appropriating the checks.

title: FCC streamlines subsea cable approvals while tightening scrutiny on foreign suppliers
blurb: The FCC adopted rules that exempt qualifying subsea cable applications from Team Telecom's lengthy security reviews in exchange for certifying to stricter standards, while imposing new licensing requirements on submarine line terminal equipment suppliers and operators. The changes accelerate buildouts to meet AI-driven capacity demand but tighten the screws on Chinese vendors, extending the agency's Covered List bans and presumption-of-denial policy for foreign-adversary-affiliated applicants.

title: Panmnesia boosts CXL scale with fabric switching. Meta repurposes old DRAM with CXL
blurb: Panmnesia will present at ISCA 2026 a next-stage CXL controller and a Port-Based Routing fabric switch that it says removes the latency penalty that kept directly attached multi-headed devices the norm, enabling larger pooled memory at stable performance. Separately, Meta is deploying CXL to attach reclaimed older DRAM to new servers, raising memory capacity and throughput without buying additional modules.

title: HPE updates Slingshot 400 switches to enable ‘sovereign’ multi-tenant AI research
blurb: HPE updated the host software and interconnect stack for its Slingshot 400 switches to let operators carve a single supercomputer into securely isolated tenants, using hardware-level MAC learning to partition routing and extending the same multi-tenancy controls to its E2000 Cray storage systems. Shared at ISC 2026 and available to existing Slingshot 400 deployments, the update targets national labs running sovereign AI workloads on HPE's GX5000 platform.

title: European telco giant wins $12M after Huawei rip-and-replace 'expropriation'
blurb: A Danish court ordered the state to pay TDC Net 80 million kroner ($12M) in compensation for the forced removal of its Huawei DWDM network, ruling the 2023 security order amounted to expropriation. The court upheld the state's right to ban the gear on national security grounds, setting a precedent that operators across Europe may cite to recover rip-and-replace costs as EU Toolbox enforcement tightens.

title: GSMA 'playbook' calls on LEO satellite providers to play by mobile's rules
blurb: The GSMA published a 44-page regulatory playbook arguing that LEO satellite providers offering direct-to-user service, including Starlink, Amazon Leo, AST SpaceMobile, and Eutelsat, should face the same consumer-protection, security, and tax obligations as mobile operators, plus local-establishment rules requiring a legal presence in each market. The trade group conceded the enforcement mechanism could amount to a single GDPR-style in-country representative, leaving the framework's accountability and law-enforcement oversight goals thinly backed.

title: Meeting Trump's 2030 Quantum Deadline Will be Expensive, Complex
blurb: Two June 22 executive orders compress the federal post-quantum cryptography timeline, requiring agencies to name PQC migration leads within 30 days, deploy PQC encryption by the end of 2030, and PQC digital signatures for high-value systems by 2031. The mandate reaches federal contractors, who must meet NIST's PQC standards by December 31, 2030. The author argues that visibility gaps across multivendor IT and OT estates, misaligned update cycles, and interoperability problems will make compliance both costly and operationally complex.

title: pgEdge joins rush to merge OLTP and OLAP storage to support AI
blurb: pgEdge introduced ColdFront in beta, a PostgreSQL-native data tiering architecture that automatically moves older records into Apache Iceberg object storage while keeping PostgreSQL as the only interface applications touch, with queries on historical data executed transparently via DuckDB. The launch puts pgEdge against Databricks' LTAP, EDB's converged analytics, and Snowflake's pg_lake in the race to collapse OLTP and OLAP silos for AI workloads, differentiated by treating Iceberg as a storage tier rather than a separate query surface.

title: Terraform MCP server: Four real-world AI infrastructure patterns
blurb: HashiCorp published a guide to four production patterns for its Terraform MCP server, which feeds AI agents authoritative context from real Terraform modules, policies, and workspaces instead of relying on model training data. The patterns cover no-code self-service provisioning, governance automation, day-two operations, and developer productivity, with the grounding aimed at cutting hallucinations and keeping AI-driven infrastructure changes inside existing compliance and cost guardrails.

title: Security Profiles Operator v1: Stable APIs, Security Hardened, and Shaping Upstream Kubernetes
blurb: The Security Profiles Operator reached v1.0.0, graduating all eight of its CRD APIs to stable and backing the release with a third-party security audit, a full hardening cycle, and a zero-downtime migration path from every prior API version. The project lets teams manage seccomp, SELinux, and AppArmor profiles as Kubernetes custom resources, recording them from live workloads and binding them to pods declaratively rather than maintaining kernel profiles by hand.

title: Securing CI/CD for an open source project, part 3: Credentials, verification, and what’s next
blurb: The final post in Cilium's CI/CD hardening series covers credential isolation and release provenance. CI credentials can push only to the development registry (quay.io/cilium/*-ci), while production push secrets sit behind a GitHub protected environment that requires explicit maintainer approval, so a compromised CI workflow can ship a malicious -ci image but never overwrite a real release tag. Every released image is signed with Sigstore Cosign using keyless OIDC and ships with an SBOM, leaving no long-lived signing keys to steal.

title: From query to action: Introducing SQL alerting in Cloud Monitoring Observability Analytics
blurb: Google Cloud added SQL alerting to Cloud Monitoring's Observability Analytics, letting teams write analytical queries over logs and traces—calculating error percentages, analyzing high-cardinality dimensions, or JOINing logs and traces—and turn them into alerting policies that fire on a row-count or boolean condition. Queries run on a schedule with a lookback window and route incidents to email, Slack, or PagerDuty, but executions bill through BigQuery at standard on-demand or reservation pricing.

title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
blurb: CVE-2026-46331, a publicly exploited out-of-bounds write in the Linux kernel's act_pedit traffic-control action, lets an unprivileged user poison the cached copy of a setuid binary like /bin/su in memory and open a root shell while file-integrity checks stay clean. The attack needs act_pedit loadable and unprivileged user namespaces open, conditions present by default on RHEL 10 and Debian 13, which Red Hat rates important.

title: Panmnesia boosts CXL scale with fabric switching. Meta repurposes old DRAM with CXL
blurb: Panmnesia will present a next-stage CXL controller and fabric switch with Port-Based Routing at ISCA 2026, arguing that switched CXL can scale memory pools without the latency penalty that kept teams pinned to directly attached multi-headed devices. Its controller shares buffers across layers to cut synchronization overhead. Separately, Meta is using CXL to graft retired DRAM onto new servers, raising per-node memory capacity and throughput while extending the life of hardware that would otherwise be written off.

title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
blurb: JFrog Security Research published the first working exploit for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel flaw where two packet-cloning helpers drop the flag marking memory as file-backed, letting a local user overwrite a binary like /usr/bin/su in the page cache and gain root. The patch landed in mainline May 21, but multi-tenant servers, CI runners, and Kubernetes hosts that allow unprivileged user namespaces remain exposed until they update.

title: OpenAI told Cannes it is “clearly in the advertising business now.” The numbers say it is barely in the door.
blurb: OpenAI made its Cannes Lions debut pitching ChatGPT as a high-intent ad platform, but its launch CPM collapsed from $60 to $25 in ten weeks even as it projects $100B in ad revenue by 2030. Sponsored links now run in seven countries across travel, retail, health, beauty, and finance, while Anthropic and Google keep their chatbots ad-free for now.

title: OpenAI Makes Its Advertising Pitch at Cannes Festival
blurb: OpenAI pitched advertising inside ChatGPT to brand and agency buyers at Cannes Lions, positioning the chatbot as a new placement channel alongside Google and Meta. The move opens a paid surface in front of ChatGPT's roughly 800M weekly users, but raises unresolved questions about ad formats, measurement, and how sponsored answers affect trust in AI-generated responses.

title: Starbucks Pilots TikTok Creator Network Program With Ad Revenue Sharing for Barista Creators
blurb: Starbucks will pilot TikTok's new Creator Network this summer, becoming the first brand to use the feature to share creative briefs with employee creators and pay baristas through ad revenue sharing. The move turns employee-generated content into paid infrastructure, a timely bet given Sprout Social data showing 61% of Gen Z frequently learn about products through EGC and 61% of consumers think brands should compensate staff who promote them.

title: Podcast Retargeting Outperforms Standard Campaigns by Up to 7x as YouTube Simulcasting Reaches New Highs
blurb: Podcast retargeting campaigns drove a 7.4x higher visitor rate (0.52% vs 0.07%) and 5.5x higher purchase rate than standard podcast buys, according to Podscribe's Q2 2026 benchmark of 34B impressions across 98,000-plus campaigns. Even with lower incrementality (12% vs 24%), retargeting still produced roughly 2.75x more incremental conversions per impression. YouTube simulcasting hit 82% of episodic campaigns, and channels with all-U.S. subscribers redeemed promo codes at more than double the rate of channels with only 25% domestic reach.

title: YouTube Shorts is getting 2x speed and a cleaner view, but it’s also ditching the dislike button
blurb: YouTube rolled out four updates to Shorts, adding a Clear Screen mode, 2x playback speed, easier muting, and a viewing timer, while replacing the thumbs-up with a heart and removing the dislike button entirely. For marketers, losing the dislike signal cuts one of the clearest negative-feedback inputs on Shorts, pushing creators to read retention and "Not Interested" taps instead of vote ratios to gauge low-effort content.

title: TikTok’s road to becoming a super app
blurb: TikTok is expanding beyond short video into a super-app model, adding TikTok Shop, a FIFA World Cup sports hub, and TikTok GO for in-app hotel and attraction booking, while pursuing a fintech license. For marketers, commerce, sports, and travel discovery increasingly happen inside TikTok itself, making the platform a closed-loop channel where awareness and conversion can run without a handoff to third-party sites.

title: YouTube Shorts is getting 2x speed and a cleaner view, but it’s also ditching the dislike button
blurb: YouTube rolled out four updates to Shorts, adding a Clear Screen mode, 2x playback speed, easier muting, and a self-limiting timer, while also swapping the thumbs-up for a heart and removing the dislike button entirely. Three of the four useful additions already exist in Instagram Reels, and dropping dislikes strips out one of the few signals viewers had to push back on low-effort content, leaving "Not Interested" and channel-level controls as the alternatives.

title: Notion kills its Gmail client after AI agents keep humans from troubling inbox
blurb: Notion shut down its standalone Gmail client after finding that AI agents now triage and handle enough inbound mail that most users rarely opened the human-facing inbox. The move signals where assistant-heavy products are heading: when an agent does the routine work, the UI built for manual review becomes dead weight, and PMs should expect to justify every human-facing surface against an agent alternative.

title: Base Resumes Block Production After Roughly Two-Hour Mainnet Halt
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated Ethereum L2 holding more than $4B in TVL, halted block production for roughly two hours Thursday after a consensus fault sequenced an invalid block and stalled the chain past height 47,806,542. The incident revived scrutiny of the single-sequencer design common to major rollups, where user funds and Ethereum settlement stay intact but liveness depends entirely on one operator.

title: Spain says ‘no exceptions or extensions’ for Binance, other crypto firms ahead of MiCA deadline
blurb: Spain's securities regulator, the CNMV, said it will grant no exceptions or extensions to Binance and other crypto firms that miss the December 30 deadline to obtain MiCA authorization. Firms operating without a license after the cutoff must cease services to Spanish customers, ending the transitional grandfathering period that let unregistered providers continue under prior national rules.

title: Ondo Finance Launches 24/7 Minting and Redemption for Tokenized US Stocks and ETFs
blurb: Ondo Finance enabled 24/7 minting and redemption for tokenized US stocks and ETFs on Ethereum and BNB Chain, removing the prior weekday-only constraint tied to US market hours and applying first to six instruments including SPYon, NVDAon, and TSLAon. Ondo Global Markets now lists over 430 tokenized assets and became the first such platform to pass $1B in TVL, with Solana support and AI-agent access via Virtuals coming next.

title: Bipartisan senators call for CFTC investigation into Polymarket after fake bets report
blurb: Senators John Curtis (R-Utah) and Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) asked the CFTC to investigate Polymarket after a Wall Street Journal report found the platform paid creators to stage fake bets, including faked wins on replica sites across 1,105 videos worth $1.9M in unreal wagers. The bipartisan letter questions whether the agency can regulate prediction markets, a sector that has ballooned alongside Polymarket's $15B valuation.

title: Tokenization firm Securitize eyes early July NYSE debut with $400 million SPAC deal
blurb: Securitize will begin trading on the NYSE under ticker SECZ next week after its merger with Cantor Equity Partners II nets roughly $400 million in gross proceeds, with fewer than 30% of SPAC holders redeeming shares. The tokenization firm issues BlackRock's BUIDL fund, now near $3.1 billion, and provides infrastructure for Apollo, KKR, Hamilton Lane, and VanEck across a $22.5 billion RWA market.

title: Tether's USDT Closes In on Ether for No. 2 Crypto Spot by Market Cap
blurb: Tether's USDT has drawn level with ether at roughly $186.1B against ether's $188.9B, putting a dollar-pegged token within a fraction of a percent of the No. 2 crypto market-cap spot for the first time. USDT supply has nearly doubled in two years to record territory while ether dropped about 24% over the past 30 days, narrowing the gap from both directions.

title: Securitize expects to raise $400 million as tokenization firm nears public debut
blurb: Securitize, the BlackRock- and Ark-backed tokenization infrastructure provider, expects to raise about $400 million by merging with Cantor Fitzgerald-backed SPAC Cantor Equity Partners II, with trading set to begin July 1 on the NYSE under the ticker SECZ. The listing arrives as tokenized real-world assets, excluding stablecoins, top $30 billion, with Securitize already powering issuance for Apollo, KKR, and VanEck.

title: Base suffers second mainnet stall in two days
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated L2, halted block production for the second time in two days, with Friday's stall lasting roughly 38 minutes after Thursday's two-hour outage tied to an invalid block choking the sequencer. No user funds were at risk in either incident, though both interrupted withdrawals and required node operators to restart their mainnet nodes to resume syncing.

title: Binance Tells EU Users It Will Wind Down Services as MiCA Deadline Hits
blurb: Binance has begun telling EU users in France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will wind down services after failing to secure a MiCA license before the July 1 deadline, halting new registrations and restricting access. The exchange withdrew its Greek application Wednesday and says it will pursue authorization in another member state, joining the roughly 83% of EU crypto providers still unlicensed under the framework.

title: Duffy’s Last Dance
blurb: CME chairman Terry Duffy faces the end of his tenure as perpetual futures, the never-expiring contracts long dominant on crypto exchanges, push toward US regulatory approval under a CFTC now openly skeptical of incumbents. The piece frames the fight as a structural threat to CME's expiring-contract model, which clears 28.1 million contracts a day and anchors its near-monopoly in US rate and equity derivatives.

title: DraftKings launches its own prediction markets exchange as consumer volume exceeds $3 billion
blurb: DraftKings launched its own prediction markets exchange, DKeX, built on the CFTC-licensed Railbird technology it acquired in October 2025. The vertical has reached roughly $3.4 billion in annualized consumer volume and $11.3 billion in total trading volume as of June 21, putting DraftKings in direct competition with Polymarket and Kalshi as World Cup activity drives the sector.

title: Framework Ventures raises $400 million for fourth fund to invest across crypto, AI and robotics
blurb: Framework Ventures raised a $400 million fourth fund, FVIV, to keep backing stablecoins, tokenization, and digital assets while expanding into AI, robotics, energy, and fintech. The oversubscribed fund writes $1 million to $50 million checks from pre-seed through Series A, and has already led Mecka AI's $60 million Series A and backed energy network Daylight.

title: Trump Blocks Housing Bill Signing Over Voter ID Demand, Putting CBDC Ban in Limbo
blurb: Trump canceled the signing ceremony for the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, conditioning his signature on Congress first passing unrelated voter-ID legislation and leaving the bill's four-year ban on a Fed-issued CBDC unsigned. The measure cleared the Senate 85-5 and House 358-32, veto-proof margins that let it become law automatically after 10 days regardless of the stalled ceremony.

title: Securities Probe Lands on Strategy as MSTR Sinks and STRC Slips Below Par
blurb: Rosen Law Firm opened a securities investigation into Strategy Inc., examining whether the company misled investors across its capital stack, including MSTR common shares and all four series of preferred stock. The notice landed as MSTR fell roughly 8% to about $86, a two-year low down 78% on the year, while STRC slipped to around $76, some 24% below par and tightening one of Strategy's main Bitcoin funding channels.

title: Kalshi Seeks $40B Valuation Seven Weeks After $22B Raise
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise fresh capital at roughly a $40B valuation, according to the Financial Times, nearly double the $22B mark it locked in seven weeks ago when Coatue led a $1B round. The repricing tracks a volume surge to over $17B in monthly trading and stretches its lead over Polymarket, which sought a $15B valuation in April.

title: Sophon Shuts Down Its zkSync Chain and Rebuilds as a Consumer App Studio on Base
blurb: Sophon is decommissioning its zkSync-based Layer 2 and relaunching as Soph+, a consumer product studio building exclusively on Coinbase's Base, after the chain raised $60M through 2024 node sales but settled at roughly 100 to 200 daily active users and $30 in daily fees. SOPH trades near $0.0048, down about 90% from its token generation event, with revenue from new products like the Pyre payment card earmarked to buy back and burn the token.

title: Crypto’s ETF Boom Gets $4.5 Billion Reality Check in Brutal Week
blurb: Crypto ETFs recorded $4.5B in net outflows over the past week, the largest weekly redemption since the products launched and a reversal of the institutional inflows that had anchored Bitcoin's price. The exits undercut the thesis that ETF buyers would dampen volatility, with spot Bitcoin funds bearing the bulk of the withdrawals.

title: Strategy Executives Issue Coordinated Investor Reassurances as STRC Hits Record Lows
blurb: Three Strategy executives, including Michael Saylor, posted coordinated investor reassurances on X Friday as the company's STRC preferred shares fell to record lows near $73, roughly 25% below par, and bitcoin traded around $59,600. The executives stressed that Strategy borrowed against its equity stack rather than its 847,363 BTC, leaving no bitcoin-collateralized loans that could force liquidations or margin calls during the drawdown.

title: Spain says ‘no exceptions or extensions’ for Binance, other crypto firms ahead of MiCA deadline
blurb: Spain's securities regulator, the CNMV, said it will grant no exceptions or extensions to Binance and other crypto firms that miss the December 30 deadline to obtain MiCA authorization. Firms operating without a license after the cutoff must halt services to Spanish customers, raising the stakes for exchanges still navigating the EU's harmonized licensing regime across member states.

title: Tokenization firm Securitize eyes early July NYSE debut with $400 million SPAC deal
blurb: Securitize said it expects to begin trading on the NYSE under ticker SECZ next week, securing roughly $400 million through its merger with SPAC Cantor Equity Partners II after fewer than 30% of holders redeemed. The tokenization infrastructure firm issues BlackRock's $3.1 billion BUIDL Treasury fund and works with Apollo, KKR, and VanEck, positioning the listing as a bet on institutional adoption of tokenized real-world assets.

title: Bipartisan senators call for CFTC investigation into Polymarket after fake bets report
blurb: Bipartisan senators John Curtis and Adam Schiff asked the CFTC to investigate Polymarket after a Wall Street Journal report found the $15B prediction market paid creators to stage roughly $1.9M in fake winning bets across its platform. The letter to Chair Michael Selig questions whether the agency is equipped to police prediction markets, escalating regulatory risk for a sector the CFTC is already reportedly probing.

title: Base Resumes Block Production After Roughly Two-Hour Mainnet Halt
blurb: Base, Coinbase's Layer 2, halted block production for roughly two hours on Thursday after a consensus fault caused an invalid block to be sequenced, stalling the chain at height 47,806,542 before sequencing resumed and funds stayed secure throughout. The outage renewed scrutiny of the single centralized sequencer that orders transactions on most major rollups, a liveness risk Base has been working to dilute through its Azul multi-proof upgrade and push toward Stage 2 decentralization.

title: Ondo Finance Launches 24/7 Minting and Redemption for Tokenized US Stocks and ETFs
blurb: Ondo Finance enabled 24/7 minting and redemption for tokenized US stocks and ETFs on Ethereum and BNB Chain, starting with six instruments including SPYon, NVDAon, and TSLAon and removing the prior weekday-only constraint on creating and exiting positions. The move gives institutional and qualified investors a full lifecycle for tokenized equities on infrastructure that never closes, and Ondo Global Markets, now past $1B in TVL, claims a first ahead of rivals limiting continuous access to secondary trading.

title: Securitize expects to raise $400 million as tokenization firm nears public debut
blurb: Securitize, the BlackRock- and Ark-backed tokenization infrastructure provider, expects to raise about $400 million by merging with a Cantor Fitzgerald-backed SPAC and listing on the NYSE under the ticker SECZ around July 1. The debut tracks a tokenized real-world-asset market that has passed $30 billion excluding stablecoins, with Securitize already powering issuance for Apollo, KKR, and the NYSE's own tokenized-securities platform.

title: Tether's USDT Closes In on Ether for No. 2 Crypto Spot by Market Cap
blurb: Tether's USDT has drawn nearly level with ether as the second-largest cryptocurrency by market cap, sitting at roughly $186.1B against ether's $188.9B and briefly overtaking it earlier this month, per CoinGecko. USDT supply has roughly doubled over two years on exchange, payments, and offshore dollar demand, even as ether fell about 24% in 30 days, showing how stablecoin issuance now rivals the base assets of the chains it settles on.

title: Binance Tells EU Users It Will Wind Down Services as MiCA Deadline Hits
blurb: Binance has begun notifying users in France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will wind down EU services after failing to secure MiCA authorization before the July 1 deadline, having withdrawn its Greek application on Wednesday. The exit reflects a wider compliance gap, with ESMA noting roughly 83% of EU crypto-asset providers still lack the single national license that passports across all 27 member states.

title: Base suffers second mainnet stall in two days
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated Layer 2, halted block production for the second time in two days, with Friday's stall resolved in under 40 minutes after Thursday's invalid-block sequencer choke took roughly two hours to clear. No user funds were at risk in either incident, though withdrawals were interrupted and node operators had to restart their mainnet nodes, raising reliability questions days after the network's Beryl hardfork.

title: Duffy’s Last Dance
blurb: Net Interest profiles Terry Duffy, CME's longtime chairman, whose climb from the lean hog pit to running a derivatives powerhouse trading 28.1 million contracts a day now faces its biggest threat: perpetual futures, the crypto-native contracts that never expire. With a CFTC chairman openly siding with the upstarts, the piece frames a fight over whether CME's near-monopoly in US rate and equity derivatives survives the shift to round-the-clock, settlement-free trading.

title: DraftKings launches its own prediction markets exchange as consumer volume exceeds $3 billion
blurb: DraftKings launched its own prediction markets exchange, DKeX, built on the CFTC-licensed technology it acquired with Railbird in October 2025. The vertical has reached roughly $3.4B in annualized consumer volume and $11.3B in total trading volume as of June 21, with the World Cup driving activity and DraftKings now competing directly with Kalshi, Polymarket, and Coinbase.

title: Securities Probe Lands on Strategy as MSTR Sinks and STRC Slips Below Par
blurb: Rosen Law Firm opened a securities investigation into Strategy Inc., examining whether the Bitcoin treasury company misled investors across its capital stack, as MSTR fell roughly 8% to a two-year low near $86 and its STRC preferred slipped to about $76, 24% below par. The sub-par STRC matters because Strategy can no longer issue preferred at face value to fund Bitcoin buys, choking a primary funding channel already strained by its first BTC sale since 2022.

title: President Trump threatens to impose a 100% tariff on any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies (Kevin Breuninger/CNBC)
blurb: President Trump threatened to impose a 100% tariff on any country that levies a digital services tax on US technology and payments companies. The threat raises the stakes for fintechs operating in markets like the UK, France, and Canada, where DST regimes already tax local revenue and could now trigger retaliatory trade measures that reshape cross-border expansion economics.

title: Kalshi Seeks $40B Valuation Seven Weeks After $22B Raise
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise fresh capital at a roughly $40B valuation, according to the Financial Times, nearly double the $22B mark it locked in seven weeks ago when Coatue led a $1B round with Morgan Stanley, Sequoia, and Andreessen Horowitz. The repricing rewards a status its rival lacks: Kalshi operates as a CFTC-registered exchange, drawing institutional backers even as the CFTC fights nine states over who regulates event contracts.

title: Sources: Donald Trump Jr. got ~$300K in Kalshi equity after becoming a strategic adviser in 2025, when Kalshi was valued at ~$2B; now it's worth $22B+ (George Steer/Financial Times)
blurb: Donald Trump Jr. received roughly $300K in Kalshi equity after joining the prediction-market platform as a strategic adviser in 2025, when the company was valued at about $2B, according to the Financial Times. Kalshi has since been valued at $22B+, an 11x markup that turned the advisory stake into a paper gain near $3.3M and underscores the political ties now woven into regulated event-contract markets.

title: Why Navan Is Up +30% in 2026. When So Many Other Public Software Leaders Are Down.
blurb: Navan, the corporate travel and expense platform formerly TripActions, posted Q1 FY2027 revenue of $220M up 40% year-over-year with non-GAAP operating margin of 11%, then raised full-year guidance to roughly 30% growth even as the iShares software ETF sits down 15% on the year. Morgan Stanley called it one of its strongest software prints, lifting Navan to a 52-week high of $24.50 after the stock had cratered 68% from its IPO price.

title: SpaceX Bears Brace for Billions in Passive Index-Fund Buying
blurb: SpaceX shares have cooled since their IPO, but short sellers are about to collide with a mechanical tailwind as the stock qualifies for major indexes like the S&P 500. Index inclusion forces passive funds to buy billions of dollars in shares regardless of valuation, a structural bid that can squeeze bearish positions and decouple the price from fundamentals for the foreseeable future.

title: Elon Musk’ Starlink could soon offer mobile services as a US carrier
blurb: SpaceX told investors it is considering a retail Starlink mobile service in the US, selling plans directly to consumers rather than filling carrier coverage gaps as it does today. The move would put Starlink in direct competition with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile, building on the wireless spectrum it acquired from EchoStar last year, though some analysts read it as leverage for better revenue-sharing deals rather than a full carrier launch.

title: Sources: Revolut told new hires they'll have to work in office at least three days a week from next year, retreating from its long-held remote-first approach (Financial Times)
blurb: Revolut told new hires they will need to work in the office at least three days a week starting next year, walking back the remote-first policy the company has maintained for years. The shift aligns the $45B fintech with a broader return-to-office push among large employers, and signals tighter operational control as Revolut scales its headcount and pursues banking licenses across new markets.

title: Crypto’s ETF Boom Gets $4.5 Billion Reality Check in Brutal Week
blurb: Bitcoin ETFs bled roughly $4.5B in net outflows over a single week, the largest sustained exit since the spot products launched and a sign that institutional holders are reducing exposure rather than anchoring it. The selloff undercuts the thesis that ETF inflows would dampen Bitcoin's volatility, leaving the asset more exposed to the same flows that were supposed to stabilize it.
